This is an "it depends". VTOL VR is a really nice VR flight/combat simulator, especially with its DLC ..... BUT (and it's a big but) it doesn't support joysticks/HOTAS and that unfortunately killed it for me. I really don't like holding my hands in the air on (for me) wonky virtual controls. 

It's a real pity as it ticks so many other boxes. I've read that HOTAS controls are not supported as they'd give some players an advantage in PVP but I'd have thought that lobbies could have an option that allows or dis-allows physical controls. I personally only play single player and co-op and it wouldn't be an issue there.

If you're happy with virtual controls then I'd say go for it but the lack of HOTAS support means a no from me.

[h1] REsh1iting 3D [/h1]

This game, apparently, is a reimagining of the previous project by the same developer — Ball 2D: Soccer Online: 

https://steamcommunity.com/profiles/76561199217001154/recommended/703940?snr=1_5_9__402

Only back then, in a 2D space, there were various mini-game interpretations besides regular soccer, whereas here, things have taken a couple of steps back, even though there's now a 3D environment.  

The idea is the same: we control, you could say, pucks rather than players, on a small field. Now we can play at most ONLY soccer. The controls aren’t too complicated, and you can get the hang of shooting and goalkeeping, but the game can’t really be called either easy to pick up or super addictive and engaging.

It's a game based on the famous wargame Warhammer, but honestly, I wasn't convinced.
First of all, you can't adjust the difficulty, while the campaign relies too much on random rolls. Not to mention that the roguelike mode (where you only save after each roll) wasn't very successful.
If you like difficult games that rely on randomness, you'll enjoy it; otherwise, it could be a nerve-wracking experience.

This game does a great job of recreating the feel of fantasy table top war gaming, most of the time.  When it is being fun, it is very fun.  Unfortunately, the AI will randomly cheat during the single-player campaign.  The AI will sometimes move units; usually flying units, into illegal positions, like within blocking terrain or on top of a player or allied unit.  Usually the units displace back into legal positions, but not always.  The most egregious example I have seen was a routed unit fleeing on top of the corner blockage of the castle map, then displacing completely off of the playing area, and then being allowed to rally and re-enter the map.  This type of behavior undermines trust in other systems of the game that are more opaque.  Until the devs apply the same move legality checking to the AI that is enforced on the player, it is hard to recommend putting up with this game's idiosyncrasies in order to scratch that fantasy table top wargaming itch.  Which is a shame, because the devs have shown exemplary dedication to their virtual reproduction in other respects.  10 factions; with their own feel, the option to see individual troopers on the table, a branching single player campaign, and periodic rebalancing passes to address balance issues and add features.  There was enough done right here to keep me playing for 235 hours, but until the AI gets an overhaul this is going into the closet.

I really wish I could recommend this to others but I just can't. After many hours, and plenty of research and failed games, I have learned how to play and found myself enjoying the game. The game scratches a real itch for me and allows you to rebuild your own nation from the ruins of a Galactic apocalypse on a random planet. It's very detailed to a fault and there's a big learning curve. The game is not user friendly at all, the UI is horrible, the reports you have to sift through can be overwhelming, and the game can be a real slog to get through. The DLC adds water planets but no naval units. Beyond moving formations on the map, your actions in the economy, diplomacy, espionage, management, etc. are determined by a literal deck of cards you have. You get cards by allocating resources to certain administrative departments. Based on how you manage your budget, you will get certain cards. Not only are important actions limited by what cards you may or may not have, but you are also limited by RNG, your incompetent leaders, and many deeper systems and points that you don't have direct control of. The game can be incredibly fun at times but also excruciatingly slow and frustrating. I honestly hope the developer keeps updating and improving their game with DLC and updates; and I will support the developer by buying their DLC. However, even though I have enjoyed my time playing, I just can't recommend this game to others.

Also everyone is whining about the solo dev using AI art. The art has no impact on gameplay and crying about it is dumb.

Shadow Empire is a game I so badly want to love. It has a great setting. It has great ideas. It has great promise and potential. 
But it sucks.
You will end up wasting countless hours being annoyed rather than entertained.
The developer, Vic, has fallen into the common pitfall of thinking that making the game 'more challenging' for the half dozen people who have figured out the mechanics, somehow makes it better.
I like to play games to generate stories. 
If you are a person who couldn't care less about stories, or history, or anything but solving a puzzle, than you might enjoy the gamey, ludicrous, nonsensical, broken mechanics and cheating AI a whole lot more than I do.
Just a few from my latest failed attempt:
1) Demands that cannot be met;
2) Recon completely broken in the early game. Fumbling around, totally blind, being ambushed by invisible troops while they all have complete intelligence on you.
3) Every minor regards you as the enemy to the point that they while being conquered by a major they send every spare soldier they can towards your capital, despite not being at war with them.
4) Every game now starts with an impregnable non-sentient life form that makes a beeline to your capital, cannot be defeated, and will sack your city, killing your population and your leaders, as herbivores are wont to do.
5) Despite a setting for 'Spread Out' every game starts with an enemy major within 20 hexes of your capital. Latest game had literally 17,304 hexes on the Spread Out option, and on turn 6, there was the enemy Major, expanding rapidly, unhindered by supply or maddened herbivores.
6) While fighting a minor, they teleported a unit across my lines to the other side, where they had unoccupied territory cut off from their capital, then had it move to cut my supply lines. 
I could go on to number 100) and beyond, but I think I made the point.
I respect the developer, Vic, for the ambition of his design. But at some point, probably after the awful Oceania expansion which made everything much worse, he should bring in a game designer that can design a fun game to go with all of his intricate systems.

TLDR . Buy if you want super crunchy, have a high tolerance for clunky annoyance, and/or need an AFK game. Otherwise wait for QoL updates or a respectful sale.  

After 346 hours (easily 200 of that is afk) I finally switched to negative, its a fun game but it needs UI work before I recommend it to anyone I know. The price to hour return ratio is good but I the quality of the hours isn't the best, older I get the more particular I get with my gaming time and ymmv.

You are in a toxic relationship with the warlord of your civilization. The warlord will do no work, but they will randomly fuck with your plans. The warlord appointed a bunch of their buddies who can't read or write to be in charge of critical aspects of the empire. You are expected to run this govt off of sticky notes that don't say much and then go digging through the pile of papers on their desk to find the correct report with the pertinent information. 

You are not in charge, you are the executive assistant expected to keep a bunch of brain damaged idiots alive mad max circa 3025. 

A very iffy recommendation, Incredible depth, awful UI, odd personal choices. A setting that is a hyperfixations of mine. I love the setting, the golden age to dark age, technology is pretty much magic, I am writing a story on the idea, complete with artwork. I love the post apocalypse vibe, I spent a decade in the Mojave desert doing post apoc cosplay parties. 

I say all that to say I have a bias a ton of leeway, especially to what I assume is a solo dev. This is such a niche intersection of multiple interests. 

The guide for this game is something around 400 pages, I say that so you understand the depth and the detail you will need to dive into this game.

If you want crunchy this bitch will break your teeth. And a lot of if its having to navigate multiple tiny tabs with oddly laid out pages to find the relevant bit of data you need. It is weirdly the most immersion breaking aspect and a good bit of the difficulty is going to be you being too lazy/high/busy to look up what you need to know, either in the guide or in the UI. 

I want to say the game captures the logistics aspect well, it has that early empire building that is too brief but really fun. There is a ton of variety in as many different tech aspects as you can realistically want. 

It is a perfect game to waste a ton of AFK time while trying to learn the process if you are doing it round by round with time in between. 

It might be a fun game to play if you are willing to dedicate what I estimate to be a  100 hours of research and experimenting to really nail the basics. An LLM can sometimes be beneficial but they seem to be using really outdated information and I'm already in a Master's program I play this to kill time between lectures and art protects not add in yet another course that is 100% textbook. It will be a fun game if moders or the devs focus on QoL.

The game uses AI, not judging on that. I use them streamline data organization, cleaning, shit like that. The game would benefit from using it more to consolidate the reports screen and build those out.  It needs a ton of QoL, there is a large selection of variables that the dev obviously feels is important for you to know but by the time you unbury it you forgot why you were looking it up or got distracted by a different thing you need to look up. 

You don't feel like a post apocalyptic warlord, you feel like the warlords bitch who does all the work while they and the council spend most of the time partying. You'll need to handle half a dozen or so critical on down tasks per round. Most of them are going to want you to make decisions on information you have to dig down mutliple layers for. After Action reports get funneld into history before you can glimpse what is wrong, etc. 

The cards system is interesting but not well executed, having to navigate to an event, realize you should use a couple of those leader cards, navigate to those, go to the leader you hope was the right one, use the cards and have what should have worked fail anyway. The execution for cards is also very arbitrary and clunky. Like the courtesan, only works on male leaders? Why is the card gendered, I need that card, clicking through every leader I have to see if it works on any of them is annoying AF. Adding the additional units to military ones, it's such an annoying system they just collect dust. Or the fun game of are you wasting prestige on a card that, doesn't infact do shit to that person and this could have easily been communicated. 

RNGesus seems to be everywhere, and there is a negative randomness to the game that is really compounded by the general sense of "Wtf am I doing again? What just happened?" 

TLDR of my TLDR. Amazing depth, good gameplay (I think), one of the most unpleseant UI's I've used in 30+ years of gaming. Games good enough to get me still playing but I really hope the UI gets work before anymore DLCs drop. 








***Original review around 200ish hours****


It is clunky AF, you will find important information buried under reports and tabs you hadn't noticed before, the tool tips need clarified and the card system for leaders really would benefit from an overhaul. Picking the targets of the cards is tedious and overall it just needs some solid QoL patching. 

But, it's fun. The lore is generic enough you can dive right in but the dev(s) sprinkled in lore nuggets you will constantly come across that helps explain the setting more and more. It is also deliciously complex, the AI is competent enough to curb stomp me consistently and the world feels alive with the different factions, including wildlife which I thought was a great approach. 

It took me until around 200hrs (with admittedly a bunch of AFK) to really get a grasp of the early to mid game but still have a ton of areas to really figure out. 

I gotta put it down for a bit but if you're a 4x fan, like high sci-fi, exploring dead precursor civilizations and want a good hours to cost ratio return you found it. Even at $40 and using AI assets I got my monies worth.

I have experience roleplaying with LLMs and was looking forward to this, but it turns out that it is significantly worse than what I can do with an average LLM. I have only played for 2 hours (two starts), but the discrepancy to current general purpose AIs is so stark I call it quits here.
For roleplaying, in comparison to current paid-for general purpose AI's, this game is, in its standard configuration, not good. It is in fact significantly worse in several ways. 

The advantage of this game is supposed to be the gamification of a roleplay: stats, skill checks, inventory, quest lines, story. But in all those areas it doesn't work properly, getting more in the way of an experience rather than enhancing it.
World building: a detailed prompt in a general LLM will give you a much more coherent world. You are given little context, no good intro to the game, the place, the context. It basically says: you're in a room. Here is an auto-generated quest I just made up. Go.
Gamification: the UI is incredibly clunky and seems to be from the early 90's. It also doesn't do what you want it to do. For example, you arrive in a place and have an "encounter" with the guards. You are given 3-4 automated actions you can choose from, e.g. "attack", "observe", etc. But in my first encounter i had 3 options: fight, intimidate, flee. But I actually wanted to do something different, and you can edit these options, but why have a game UI if I have to type in what i want to do anyway? I got a spear out of the encounter that never showed up in my inventory. Mixing open-ended text prompts with predefined buttons doesn't work properly either, confusing the AI. I say "I leave the shack", and the AI says I say that but don't move. What? I type "I leave" again. The AI says , "on your way out (of a trader's hut) you see a chest and use your crowbar to pry it open." You what, mate?! 

The other gamifications, such as the pictures, are just terrible. I said I was a fat guy. Scenario was post-apocalypse. But instead of showing a post-apo guy with worn clothes and stuff, who is  overweight, the pictures just showed a monstrous fat guy in street clothing. And the pictures are all unrelated to what is happening in the story, more a distraction than anything. 

So honestly, just get yourself a good current AI and give it a precise prompt, and you will fare better than with this game.

The AI needs major changes before I can turn this into a yes.

Since I myself have spent thousands of hours with Gemini using my own good system prompts and additional physics and magic systems, or even pen-and-paper rulebooks I wrote myself, and have written my own front end (Hud für the chat app) for RPGs, I have to say that the idea sounds great... Unfortunately, the storytelling is really bad. It forces you into the quest even if you don't want to pursue it. Even if you've searched every room of a location multiple times and paid attention to everything... there's always something new there. 
You read a logbook cover to cover, and suddenly, information appears in it that simply wasn't there before 
You have a lighthouse and TWO!!! secret cellars inside with several rooms

as if there were no memory system behind it... (Edit: dev sayed to me there is no one)

and it's not Cthulhu, but you have to take care of your soul... and if you have a totally hardened character, the AI ignores that and you still end up with 0 soul salvation because of little things...

I just curse... The idea is so good... but the way it is, it's bad. Unfortunately.

It annoys me especially because I've played a lot and developed a really good memory system just through system prompts. I made a graphical front end for all the RPGs with a JSON storage system, etc... and then I saw it by chance as a recommendation in a Reddit forum and thought... wow, someone did a much better job than me, and the storytelling is so bad that it gives me a headache... I'm just disappointed...

Probably there are still some things that can be changed... (unfortunately not everything, such as the soul salvation system), but right now I'm angry, have a headache, and am annoyed...

I always say that if the source code is incomprehensible, it's because of the lack of documentation... and that's why I'm negative for now... let's see what else comes up.

Edit : And you can tell that I was a tester for decades... the longer I play, the more errors I notice... but it works in my own front end...

Edit2: So according to developers and fans, it's all my fault, even if things like mental stability values are part of the character and the game calculates them incorrectly, it's all my fault, and when I use it as LLM like my chats, it's all my fault too. This game is really a tragedy. It's got a great dark, gritty atmosphere with Souls-like crumbs of lore, and the slow realism of the movement lends itself well to the horror vibe of exploring a pitch black dungeon crawling with lethal undead. The centerpiece of the game is its physics-based combat system, which is incredibly unique, if rough around the edges, and creates endless opportunity for dynamic gameplay. The fights feel tense, engaging, and you're always just one wrong (or right) move away from a spectacular kill, a horrible death, or seeing a zombie lose its balance and have a seizure while wrestling with a chair. 

Unfortunately, this game's unique engine and its great potential for fun are actively sabotaged by most of its other design decisions. It seems to belong to the bizarre sub-genre of games that pride themselves on "hyper-difficult," "perma death," etc., which most of the time means being actively hostile to players and making sure you don't enjoy yourself too much.

The game has 8 dungeon levels currently, at least according to what I've read. I stopped playing on level 3, since that's where the game arbitrarily decides to stop having checkpoints, which I had to learn by dying and losing multiple floors of progress which took hours. Like many of these types of games, it adds "difficulty" by completely disrespecting your time. Losing one floor of progress is already a steep penalty, and certainly fulfills the goal of giving weight to the gameplay. I understand wanting a game to have stakes, but with how unforgivingly easy it is to die & and the near-total lack of healing items, I'm not even going to attempt to complete the remaining ~10 hours of the game without one death, restarting on floor 3 every time I do. For obvious reasons, this degree of time loss is not fun for 99% of people.

The arena mode is slightly better in this regard, but it too is confusingly designed to waste your time. It's a massive grindfest. Monetary & experience rewards are a fraction of what they should be, and you'll have to repeat dozens of nearly-identical fights to advance one fighter a single level. This wears out its welcome quickly, especially considering that the controls need a lot of work, several of the arenas are so dark that you literally can't see your weapons, foreground objects will completely block the view of the fight, and trying to land a blow with a sword is like trying to punch someone in a dream. The UI is atrocious, I had to google how to do almost anything. Some arena matches have weapon restrictions, but you can't enter them unless you actually own the weapon, which must be purchased from the 200+ weapon types from a merchant who restocks about 4 randomly generated items every day. I could go on.

There's a great game in here somewhere, but in its current state it's really not playable as more than a novelty. If you browse Steam threads so that you can write "git gud" and "This isn't a real roguelike, meta progression violates the archetype of the eponymous 1980 dungeon crawler 'Rogue,' after which the genre is named" then you'll probably love it and you can exclude yourself from this review, which is for normal people.

original review 5th of november 2025: The game is okay, unique and fun but difficult to get into. It's being developed VERY slowly, but for the pricetag, you get more than what you pay for as is, on top of it being a one-of-a-kind experience. There are issues but nothing game-ruining.
The main draw is the unique combat system, but it's quite atmospheric too, and the level design is refreshingly thoughtful.

I just don't feel comfortable supporting this game anymore, I don't mind the year(s) between updates or the incredibly vague blabbering on about whatever on their own. But the absolute hostility the developers seem to have to the community, on top of those two other points makes me feel bad about supporting this game.
Especially since recently a developer has went onto the discussions solely to mock and make fun of people, though I'm not particularly offended, it's quite disheartening and makes me doubt the direction the game is going.

The game has two main game modes, the Story, which is more about vibes than combat, quite unique and interesting but will probably turn away lots of players, being labyrinthine and "boring", with a awkward difficulty curve to boot, where it feels impossible when you're new, and disappointingly easy when you've got a good grasp on the game.
And the Arena, which is a combat focus, lots of fighting, better for easing into the game and will probably keep the interest of most players, There's not much to say, you fight things, get money buy new hirelings and gear then fight some more.

The game is quite un-intuitive, with the only real guide or tutorial being the in-game manual, which is vague, all-over-the-place and doesn't really feel all that relevant, so you mostly learn by doing.
The community sometimes answers questions but you're just as likely to be made fun of as you are to receive information or help, at least that's what I've mostly seen.

The game is fun for a while, and worth the money for a certain kind of person, but the game is only really for that kind of person, and both the communities and the developers aren't particularly welcoming.
With a steep learning curve and lack of guidance, you'll probably only get into it if you're persistent, and I'm starting to doubt it deserves support.

not gonna sugar coat it. this is one of the worst games ive ever played. the controls are so deliberately bad that any cool or interesting aspects the game does have become completely irrelevant, let me explain. 

Have you ever wonder what its like to maneuver a school bus through a dungeon? well look no further: this is basically top down QWOP, you dont control your character you drive him, A turns you left, and D turns you right and W is the gas. its a completely insane way to set up the control, and doesnt even work for combat, so you have to press TAB to switch to combat mode where you track the cursor, but this mode is impossible to get around in or interact with the world in so forget just playing in that mode. the controls are SO bad, that the game actually becomes the controls, like QWOP. the difficulty of the game is literally derived from its absurdly difficult control scheme and every other potentially interesting feature or aspect of the game becomes completely overshadowed. As far as the devs are concerned the game is a joke, and the controls are the punchline. The devs have made it clear in forum posts that they are aware the controls are regarded as awful by most people, and argue that while it is a departure from standard input conventions it only takes a few HOURS to work it out, and justify the controls they chose as superior under the pretence that once you work it out its better. while they recognise this is a departure from standard input conventions they never bother to stop themselves for a moment and ask *why* some conventions become standards. No, the devs would rather have your character stop on the spot, slowly rotate 180 degrees without moving, and laboriously move forawad again in order to simply TURN AROUND AND RUN AWAY FROM SOMETHING. it is LUDICROUS to suggest that moving your PC around like a damn TANK is a good idea, no other game in history has ever had to 2 seperate input configurations in order to implement combat, and the very fact that changing those inputs for combat is the devs subconsciously admitting "Yikes guys, i dont think the input scheme we have is workable for combat, were gonna have to include something else" yeah, no shit! its not usable for ANYTHING let alone combat.

there is NO way to heal, aside from a single healing potion on each level, which doesnt even fully heal you, and getting hit a single time can spell death, or rather usually mortally wound you making the rest of the level impossible, it requires you to be flawless from the start, or you simply wont be able to make it through to the end. 

the game also includes a terribly implemented magic system, completely illusive to the player at the begining of the game, and likely completely useless untill the mid to late game, i wouldnt know, i didnt get past the 3rd level so the only spell i ever cast just detected npc's through walls.

I've been playing 4x since the original Sid Meier's Civilization.  Played pretty much every one and enjoyed them.  I came to this one because I like to play games after their launch and shake down cruise and the Holiday sale.

Gal Civ 4 feels like, "What if we did Civ in space?  Instead of land to upgrade, you can build starbases between planets."  So it's great; it's a highly polished version (4th generation) of Civilization in space.  A better tech tree, AI, more customizable units, more strategies, diverse races some with missing mechanics to make their approach divergent, more mechanics to try out and explore ... and on and on.  My hold back here is that the game feels like it has stayed the same more than it has evolved.  With three decades, I'm hoping for something new.  Also, I'm not as young as was several decades ago, so I don't have time to micromanage units and cities/planets.  Frankly, it was always a slog with 4x, but with interstellar distances being vast, most of my time is spent planning to move units.  And then of course, there is the ship customizer for you to micromanage your fleet designs.   And what about setting up starbase array of multiple starbases across your territory and manage their modules so your ships can travel as quickly as possible?  It's a lot of micromanaging, and at my point in life, too much.  Like the Civ of Yor, I'm 18 hours into a game and the end is not in sight.  Games are longer than the counterparts.  Another point is that at my age, I don't have the time to play multiple 40 hour play throughs.

But hey, maybe you will read this review and say you're a big fan of getting lost in micromanaging an entire galactic civilization.  To each his own.

UPDATE: I'm sure everyone is looking at this and saying, how can he not recommend it if he's played 77 hours?  Well, I've played one game on large map for 50 hours, and I haven't finished the tech tree for the end game.  Every turn I have manufacturing to manage for 20 planets and then send the ships it produces into fleets.  Grind grind.  Own half the galaxy, but still not done.

Yes, my sphere of influence is growing. Yes, my fleets are getting stronger. Yes, my planets are developed and industrial/scientific powerhouses. But where does it all lead and how does it add up? There's a finite amount of fun in watching a circle grow larger, or your fleets (of miniscule size compared to competitors BTW) engaging in terrible 2d auto-combat, or clicking build for the fortieth time on the newest colony.

There are the trappings of a decent 4X scifi game, but only that and nothing more. There is no depth to the mechanics, no beauty to the "art," and certainly no polish to the systems (what even is the shipyard ship designer?).

I would not recommend. Stardock makes other decent games. GalCiv4 isn't one of them.

It's too bad but I can't recomend this game. In a nutshell: It's boring. :(

Let me explain.

First some info about my playstyle so you can put my review in perspective. I really like 4x games. I've played Master of Orion 2, GalCiv 3 and Stellaris. I have 3000 hrs (OMG!) in GalCiv 3 and in Stellaris (each!). I play in 4k on a 32" monitor on the biggest possible map with 7-10 AI-players.

Okay, I had a wall of text writen here outling my complaints but what is the point? The game has a nice UI, clean, easy to read with lot's of tooltips.

But there is no depth. There are no stories, nothing to keep me motivated. I played my second game for approx. 30 hrs, I had my sector settled with approx. 30 core planets. By the time I had reached the other sectors the other AI-players had settled their sectors. Going to war seemed pointless and boring so I quit the game.

Just quickly 2 things I really did not like.

The map:
The generated galaxy has a bunch of solar systems but about 50% of them are white stars which means there will most likely not be anything interesting there, Why?

Mods:
Uhm, there only a few of them? I clicked on the workshop in steam and sorted mods by most popular and all time and all I got were ships. Okay, on the starting screen of the game there is a mod button. Clicked that, clicked on get Mods in the following window. That opens a page in your browser with a list of mods, The list is not big, gameplay mods don't seem to exist and some of the mods have not been updated in years. What's up with that? This is much better in other games.

What I still like are the cutscenes. When you do something for the fist time, like mine a resource or settle a planet you get a nice cutscene which adds to the immersion imo. I wish other 4x games had that.

So my verdict:
If your new to this genre get Stellaris (it's not as compicated as it looks). If you're not new but haven't played it yet then get Stellaris. You've played Stellaris but not with mods then get mods (!!) and play Stellaris. No really get mods (like Gigastructures and ACOT), It'll be a whole new game! ;)

Don't get GalCiv 4 unless you want an easy and bland game. I got the game on a wintersale and I'm regretting it. ;)

I want to love this game.  I've tried real hard (177 hours.)  But, I can't.  I'm a long time Gal Civ I, II and III player, but this one misses the mark.

The game, as implemented by design choices, is simply too chaotic to be called a "strategy" game.  There are nodes scattered throughout space, you go investigate them and a random event happens.  If you do not investigate these nodes, you will lose because your opponents will investigate them.  But the random events are... random, and most importantly wild.  Things like powerful previously-non-existing civilizations appearing on your border.  You just can't meaningfully plan ahead because you don't know what chaotic thing will happen next.  One of the several "questionable" things that can come from a node is an artifact that will simply teleport an enemy fleet somewhere far away.  Since there are hundreds of these nodes, I usually have scores of these teleporters when the AI fleets finally come a knocking.  I just teleport them away and continue on with my normal game.  BTW:  You can't turn any of this off in the settings.

There are obviously critical technologies that require particular resources to implement.  If those resources aren't near you, just start over.  You CAN make resources abundant in the settings.  But what would be so much better is the ability to work around these resource shortages with alternative technology trees.  But... yeah, not in this game.

Planets you colonize and develop require governors to run them.  Governors are scarce, and expensive.  High quality governors are almost non-existent.  All this adds up to you only being able to colonize and develop a very limited number of planets.  And, this number doesn't seem to scale with Galaxy size.  You will not be creating an industrial behemoth and flooding space with massive fleets in this game.  You just can't build up the industrial base to do it.

Ship automation is horrible, just don't turn any of it on.  Manually move your scouts, transports, etc. slowly across the map lest they run head first into a pirate base (which probably just appeared this turn due to some random event.)  I mean, you HAVE to scout, quickly and wide, but you also HAVE to manually keep control of all of it or it will be dead in just a few turns.  This gets tedious quickly.

Opponent AIs are all the same, as soon as one of them finds you it tells all the other AIs and then they come at you with everything they have for the rest of the game.  (Thank goodness for the teleporter artifacts!)  This is not a 4X game, this is a war game pretending to be 4X.  There are no "friendly" AIs in this game.  They are all smart enough to get to space, but not smart enough to understand game theory, tit for tat strategy, etc.

You can take over your ships in battle, but the system is so cumbersome to use that your better off just soaking up the losses that occur when the AI controls them.

I still recommend Gal Civ II :)

Picked up on sale out of curiosity. It makes a pretty bad first impression. I'm going to keep playing a little more to see if it picks up, but I have my doubts. I'll say at the top if you're looking for a 2d platformer "lovecraftian" game with procedurally generated levels, minor roguelite elements, and a strong visual identity, go play Sundered.

The enemies and combat are quite difficult to read at first, and don't behave like most other games of similar type. Only certain parts of the paper-doll style enemies count as attacking limbs, and they glow a little when they are actively "attacking" Projectile type attacks are more conventional. But you can get piled by a heap of monsters who then cannot hit you because they ragdoll across the screen and piled up backwards, leaving their damaging parts a million miles away unable to hit you. It's very silly. They don't even manage to look scary or interesting or "incomprehensible" in a good way. Just incomprehensible in a boring way.

The hub area is sprawling, in a bad way also. There are a few rooms that might hint at mystery, maybe stuff gets unlocked and fills in the empty space eventually, but the horrible endless staircases used to traverse the area are miserable and there's no reason for the rooms to be that big, they fail to even evoke a sense of "epicness" in scale which is the only reason I can think for the layout being how it is. It feels like it's actively trying to discourage exploration.

Movement and combat, controlling the character, is deeply unpleasant and clumsy. The UI is difficult to read. The visuals while playing are quite indistinct and muddy.

The "Lovecraftian" theming is... heavy-handed, unsubtle, indulgent, and more than a little silly. It's possible this is on purpose and that's what they're going for, a bit silly and over-the-top, which is a totally fair if so, but I'm not sure that was intended, and it makes the tone feel hard to pin down. The NPC in the hub that literally says "I Wear No Mask" made me laugh out loud. it's so absurd and from nowhere. It's just pointing and saying "Hey yeah! King in Yellow reference! Isn't that great?"
Game only loaded once for me, sorta; but there were no graphics... just randomly sized hitboxes in a void. Then I tried restarting it a few times and it never loaded again... just endless loadscreen

This game is not good on many levels. Do not spend the full $20 on this, or even the sale price of $2. You're better off watching a tutorial on how to make a game in Scratch and doing it yourself.

The mechanics are ridiculous. I spent ten minutes trying to figure out the dash, which doesn't seem to follow the path of your cursor OR the keyboard. The maps have lots of areas for double jumping and shift, and you'd better hope you don't need to do the latter to progress.

The enemies look interesting but are so painfully basic. They ragdoll like Gmod (moving around the map AND when engaged) and attack with no unique move sets. The dialogue with the NPCs is so painfully basic that I stopped interacting with them unless forced to.

There are non-skippable AI art cutscenes (at least 10-15 seconds long) that have no explanation or seemingly hidden meanings. Just a slow zoom on a moving AI image of something I can only imagine is the developer typing in "creepy dark fantasy tower/landscape/monster" into an AI generator over and over again.

This game is trying so hard to be platformer Dark Souls with the font, location names, and general mystery in the "base" area where you can upgrade your stats and talk to more vague NPCs. If you prompted an AI game maker to make everything I just described, it would be this game. 

Don't waste your money. Buy a Miyazaki game for the Souls experience, and Hollow Knight for a platform one.

I have over 2,200 hours in this game, and I cannot recommend it.

The game is astronomically buggy. Some bugs have existed for years, despite multiple patches and updates claiming to fix them. These updates frequently fail to resolve longstanding issues and often introduce new bugs.

Instead of prioritizing bug fixes or stability, development focus appears to be on releasing new animals and cosmetic content, which often launch in a severely bugged state. These releases are frequently pushed out before existing issues are resolved, creating a cycle where unfinished content piles on top of unresolved problems.

Developer communication with the community is effectively nonexistent. Feedback from US/EU players is routinely ignored, and the lack of communication is often excused as a language barrier. After years of this pattern, it feels less like a barrier and more like a lack of effort to engage with the community.

Monetization is also a serious concern. While Steam DLCs are priced around $20 and less, additional animal skins and recolors are sold outside of Steam for $60–$150, despite being visually similar to existing in-game or DLC skins. This would not include Brazil, where regionally the prices for DLC's inside and out of Steam are 3x times lower. On top of that, paid skins, including Steam DLC skins, have been altered or removed after purchase, which severely undermines trust in buying any cosmetic content.

Overall, the developers do not meaningfully listen to player feedback, nor do they appear interested in finishing or improving the game in a substantial way. After years of unresolved issues and increasingly aggressive monetization, the game feels less like a passion project and more like a cash cow.

I eventually found myself either baiting enemies into a single corridor or just holding an angle instead of actually moving quickly through the level, since you need to clean out the level before being able to continue, which was getting boring and didn't seem like how the game was intended to be played.

The level design, at least in the first two levels, is really poor and I couldn’t motivate myself to push any further - especially since the game doesn't allow you to save manually but also doesn't save inbetween single levels. 

I definitely can understand where people see the appeal in these types of games but I believe there are just better alternatives that aren't this unforgiving and frustrating to play.

Dreadhunter is kinda ok now with a couple years of early access gone by, but that's all it is, and clearly that's all it is going to be.  It's a bit better if you are the type to want things to scale up to where a lot of your fighting is automatic, but still the price is punching way above what the game offers, or (from watching the project since before early access) what the devs have experience to deliver.  Its content is lush without being different or interesting, leading to samey fights, your build is eventually fancy as you let more upgrades take responsibility for you (also leading to samey fights), but building it is mainly a process of time investment and perseverance.  The level grind is glacial and comically unrewarding.  The gearing is boring, restrictive, and basically sabotages itself and its mechanics.

The enemies are each fairly one-note, boring to fight, and sometimes just really annoying as you get to later enemies.  Much of the time is spent fighting very few enemies in very few ways, which makes missions feel plodding and samey, up until the end battle which is always a neverending swarm of adds and something you're trying to get damage onto across them.  Granted, adds are required for several of the game's core mechanics to work, but this miasma of the game's few enemies does blur the big fights together in their own equally forgettable way.  There's so much forgettable time you need to invest in slow, small upgrades, but somewhere between one and two dozen hours, things start to come together enough that it's a mildly fun kind of forgettable rather than pure slog, as long as you can avoid thinking about how much time it took--and how much more time it will take--to get to whatever you were doing here.

There are a lot of pain points, especially in the builds/mechanics, that you have to just choke down and put out of mind to play, though, depending on the path you choose.

Also, you can't play the higher difficulty until you clear the entire second highest difficulty, and when you move up, you are both locked to that difficulty and the story progress is completely reset, so you have to do everything over again, but it's still the same level as before and drops the same level stuff, just enemy stats are boosted to make it harder than it was, but in no way aligned to where you come out of the previous difficulty.  Games shouldn't waste player time on artificial nonsense like that.  The back half of the campaign on hard was a waste of time, as is at least the front half of nightmare.  ARPG monster level is a well-trod issue, and this is a known poor way to solve it.  No one just wants to retread the "story" over again, especially when it's just a list of procedural missions you run down until it stops, and you have to wade back through valueless content to get to where you want to be only because the devs pretend that makes sense.

That said, I do think this has a market out there.  If you want a lighter top-down shooter where you start weak and slowly get stronger and less nuanced while running basically the same fairly nice looking missions, grinding out the occasional small upgrade, and adjusting your build (which is mostly "avoid the bad options if you can figure them out") when you feel like it rather than getting reset like in a roguelite, this game tries to walk that line, even if it significantly overstates itself on the store page.  It's like an overpriced edgelord comfort shooter, but sales may eventually line it up for you.
It's an interesting concept, and has lots of potential, but this is nowhere near worth $35.

2 Hours.

I wrote this original review at 76 minutes and was finding it frustrating. 

Your melee/ranged combat combination is not unusual. It's a staple of games across many genres, with the original DOOM as a perfect example. Diablo IV also has many classes where close in melee and ranged attack abilities overlap to allow functioning builds and play styles. So, not at all unusual.

The problem your game has here is that you run out of ammunition early on and then you just get nuked, and it is not fun. 

I have several friends who picked this game up and most of them have less then an hour of gameplay and they are all the sorts of players that will 100% a steam game on achievements before they move on, and without achievements, they will sink dozens of hours into a game before moving on and a year or so later jump back in again. 

Less then an hour of gameplay. Less then 2 for me. 

We could all have refunded the game at this point (I've played a little more so can't but that's beside the point).

What needs to be seriously considered as a change is that early on, you have better ammunition management available - even if it is on a weaker gun with significant ammunition and ammunition gain abilities, there's an imbalanace for early game first several missions gameplay where you run out of ranged weapon ammunition and melee will get you killed well before you find more ammunition to be impactful. 

Bug Report: There's a random bug that when you move near certain parts of walls or maps (I haven't figure the specifics out at this point), the game teleports you to the furthest central south part of a levels map. The first time it happened I figured it was part of the map in question, the second time it was random no enemies on screen and teleported me behind a curved computer desk that no amount of move/dash/shoot/melee-dash/mell could destroy or get me out of and I was forced to exit the mission as a result. the third time I walked out of the start area and teleported to the other side of the map, it teleported me twice from other points in the map to the same location. This is incredibly frustrating as back tracking to clear rooms or unexplored areas and even being midway through that getting teleported back to the southern central tip of the map...so yeah. This is also part of the currently negative review, but the ammunition issues early and being forced to spec into ammunition skill trees first, that's the big issue on the review here, but this bug is needing to be looked at.

I wouldn't recommend this to anyone, but I understand the appeal.

If I were to explain the gameplay of this, I would say it's "Counter Strike for people with slow reflexes." The game focuses on a slow, methodical playstyle, and making stupid decisions will send you to the garage in seconds. This explanation may be weird in 15v15 battles, but it makes much more sense in the 7v7 competitive mode, which is way better than random battles.

It's also a game of RNG; pretty much everything important depends on luck—whether you hit the enemy, whether it deals damage, how much of it, and what enemies you will be fighting, since matchmaking doesn't use SBMM. If that doesn't make you mad, the insane pay-to-win economy will. Playing without paying doesn't make any sense unless you want to keep grinding credits instead of playing fun tanks.

I am an experienced player with over 14 years of gaming experience. The game has changed significantly in recent years. It has become more casual than before, losing its charm and strategic gameplay, and now resembles an quick action FPS tank game. Artillery has been cut back so much in favor of fast light and medium tanks that I don't even know where this will lead, does not make sense to play heavy or slower tanks. I played this game for 14 years and I can now say it is completely unrecognizable. The new UI is terrible and very depressing to look at.

This is coming from a pro at the game, with a recent winrate of 68% and 3486 recent WN8 with over 10k battles played.

This game is good if you want to play with friends, but there's a lot of predatory nature's being practiced such as gambling, overcharging, and pay 2 win advantages. It's no longer really historically accurate like it used to be, but it's pretty addicting which is pretty dangerous. 

The game started good, you actually had to learn the good old fashion skill, knowledge, and experience. But once they 2020 came out, things started dying, lots of good players started leaving the game because all the stuff they learned started becoming obsolete with new mechanics like armor flashlight which shows the weaknesses in real time. I understand they wanted to make it more beginner friendly but all they did with that was make a lot of tanks that normally where less played and had an unusual weak spot, VERY hard to play.

The introduction of tanks that require no skill to play such as artillery's stun mechanic, the BZ-176, the Ares line with it's old HE mechanics that broke the game before (let me tell you they removed the old HE mechanics because it would ALWAYS do half the damage on a none pen which people kept abusing) the XM-57, the DZT, the DBV, the cobra, and the MBT-B all of which gives a very unbalanced advantage to the player. They all speed up the progression of the game WAYYY too fast, to the point nobody enjoys the game aside from the people who speeds up the game with the unfair advantage. 

Just to let you know, I know this because i've been on both sides of the table, being the person who's played those unfair tanks and the person fighting against it, so my point is fairly valid and unbiased.

Long story short, don't open your wallet unless you know for a fact you'll play longer than 4 years, play with friends so the game is actually fun and you have dependable teammates, and there's quite a bunch of learning curbs and unfair things that need to be rebalanced.

The way tanks are spotted in this game in combination with the minimap makes it not fun and mostly about staying/moving in fixed positions on every map. If you don't know the routes to take in a map you will get highlighted and visible for all the opponents tanks that will be in preferred positions and you will get punished for it. 

Want to flank? Only way to not be spotted by the enemy team and show up on their minimap is if you're lucky and the enemy made a mistake in not covering one of the camping positions every map have. If they cover them correctly, at best you will only be spotted and most of their team will know, or you will be shot and killed.

The camera being in third perspective enables the player to see behind rocks or what is happening on the other side of a wall, so when the opponent shoots you can see it from a position of no danger then move out and shoot him. 

Every map plays out that all the tanks go to the preferred routes where there is elevation/building cover, know all the positions where only your overpowered hull is visible and can't be damaged unless maybe if you buy premium ammo.

In most positions, if you shoot a shot, you will be spotted by the enemy team and visible on the minimap for most of their team, and then you have to drive back behind elevation or a rock etc and wait for 10 seconds and then you can move back to behind a bush and repeat the process again. 

This game could have been better without the spotting and minimap.

I've been playing for over 14 years, and I've been coming back a bit since the famous 2.0 update, but my opinion is still mixed, if not [b]completely negative[/b].

[h3]Pay To Win “gold” ammunition[/h3]
Some will say that it no longer costs “gold,” but game credits, and has done so for a long time.
It's a huge joke and bad faith.
This ammunition is so much more expensive that in order for a game to be financially profitable, you absolutely need the premium subscription + WoT Plus, and/or regular purchases of credits or gold with real money. For me, that's the exact definition of Pay To Win.
This ammunition completely breaks the damage blocking mechanics, especially for heavy tanks whose armor is almost useless against “gold” ammunition with its insane penetration. (Are we talking about the Maus? What a joke...)

[h3]Terrible Matchmaking (revamped in 2.0)[/h3]
It's a huge joke, even worse than I remember, with games ending in record time between 1 to 5 against 15.
It's just completely ridiculous.
Of course, it still doesn't take player skill into account, so either you get lucky (RNG) or you don't and just waste your time with a team that's less effective than the War Gaming bots they add for the lower tiers when there aren't enough players.
Not to mention that now, in premium tanks, you'll very often encounter +2 levels, whereas they were limited to +1 with the old Match Making. As a reminder, so-called “Premium” tanks are generally weaker than their standard equivalents, their strength being that they generate more experience and, above all, more credits (except for the Premium tanks “offered” by War Gaming, of course).

[h3]Toxic Community[/h3]
Players are still as toxic as ever. I've lost count of the number of insulting messages directed at the team by players who, more often than not, haven't done anything useful in the game! When they're not pushing you out of the bushes for fun, and when you use the complaint system, absolutely nothing happens.
I have to admit that they are very intelligent to have set up a system to make players believe that they can complain about inappropriate behavior, but in reality it probably does nothing at all.

[b]So basically, unless you have an account with more than 200 tanks, etc., move on.[/b]

I do not HATE this game. I think it was worth what I paid to have a bit of fun and I don't have regrets about purchasing it. What is REALLY cool about this game is the sheer amount of monsters and sprites, and fusing said monsters to create your perfect little killing machine with the coolest traits and finding strong synergies yourself.

That being said, the combat doesn't exactly feel thrilling or satisfying once you have your fusion and spells and what not. It's just a lot of the same over and over again- go through the level pressing 'A' on every object, get into a fight and press 'A' until you win. It has the concept and beginnings of what could be an amazingly fun game, fusing your monsters at the core of that. It just wasn't all that fun to actually PLAY once you have your monsters.

I really, REALLY tried to get excited about this game, but the "fusion" the game boasts of is one of your monsters changing colors, and the other just dissapearing. No "Gekko+bird=quetzkotl" more like "Centaur+hell hound=red Centaur." There MIGHT be more to the game than this, but I just couldn't get invested enough to care.

[h3]60 FPS Limit? Yes

Do you recommend this game? No[/h3]

Almost good, but ultimately grindy beyond belief (or anything close to fun) and by a dev with an attitude issue that seems to think they're smarter than they actually are.

The game touts thousands of hours of gameplay, but it's really just repeatedly dragged out for hours by the excessive amount of grinding required for certain mats to advance questlines or progression elements to build your base. 

Sure, you can summon and fuse thousands of monsters or play different classes, but after the first few floors, it's trivial to come up with a team with any class where you'll likely just be holding down auto attack 99.99% of the time in combat, which is the bulk of those thousands of hours. Since enemies are basically just giant hp bags with one gimmick each and each fight is just beating down groups of the same enemies over and over, it gets super repetitive really quickly. You can change the difficulty if you want, but you see no difference in anything other than monster stats (no reward bonus or anything), so why bother?

There's also tons of equipment and spell crafting systems you could try out, but ultimately most of it is all meaningless due to the lack of difficulty minus a few bosses here or there if you're unlucky or just not good at team building. Sure you can get a bunch of spells and upgrade equipment, but when you can just brute force your way through, again, why bother?

Every little meaningless story drip requires clearing 3 levels of procedural dungeons that are littered with pickups. Would be tolerable if there was an auto explore system, but for reasons only they understand, the dev refuses to add one. So have fun walking all over big square maps looking for whatever piles of foozles it wants you to grab next. Oh, and when it decides to drip more story at you, prepare to deal with lots of 'funny' edgelord-style writing as your reward for doing it.

The game touts crossover play, but again for reasons only they understand, the dev has come up with a system with player controlled id codes to 'save' your game across platforms. Basically you put in a sequence of numbers and this is supposed to allow you to recover your game on another platform. However, since they are non unique ID's and not assigned by the game, any other player could input the same code and decide to delete your data when they think they are seeing 'garbage data'. Don't worry though, instead of actually fixing the system the dev just added a big red letter warning saying they aren't responsible for lost data. Feel like losing those hundreds or thousands of hours of gameplay because you wanted cross platform saves? The dev doesn't care and blames you for using their system.

Oh, and if you don't like how they do anything, they'll just ban you from the community forums here. I got banned for daring to say that the dev should post patch notes on here instead of discord only. Their response was to ban me permanently from the community forums and declare they weren't changing because they "didn't want to clog the steam feed with notes 99% of people would never read anyway" as if they have never heard of a sticky post on the forums..

As it is, check it out on heavy discount at best or just go play a decent monster summoning game from a dev without ego issues instead.

This is less of a game than it is a grind for the purpose of grinding. The core idea of this game is really cool in theory: You play a monster tamer that collects a team of 6 monsters to fight other teams of 6 monsters (6v6 at once, this is more Dragon Quest Monsters than Pokemon). Then on top of that, each monster has a special unique passive, some of which synergize this combo. On its own neither of these factors are terribly unique, but the third tentpole of the game is the idea that your character himself has unique passive abilities that you can earn by collecting perk points. The objective here is to ultimately build a self sustaining engine where your character's abilities synergize with your monster abilities, enabling you to break the game by stacking crazy amounts of damage and ripping enemies apart. As an example, I played as a pyromancer that can set enemies on fire, stack burn debuffs and use them for increasingly potent attacks. I was able to eventually put together a combination of abilities that would 1.Burn all of my allies (they heal instead of taking damage from burning) and my enemies. 2. cast a spell that hits every enemy and recasts itself for each burning enemy (of which there are usually 5 or 6) 3. have another monster who automatically attacks any monster that gets hit with a spell. This results in an engine where the game essentially plays itself. That, in itself, is pretty cool. 

The problem is the rest of the game outside of that loop is pretty wretched. The gameplay outside combat is essentially exploring procedurally generated RPG maker quality dungeons, fighting enemies to gain experience and looting random chests off the ground as the game shovels massive amounts of different currencies and various (mostly useless) items into your mostly indifferent mouth. The writing is perhaps some of the worst I've ever seen in a game, where instead of putting effort into writing a story the game decides that the fact that it is poorly written is a joke in and of itself. You get quests with names like "generic RPG fetch quest" and frequently characters will break the fourth wall to remind you how much all of this sucks. But I could handle all of that if there was actually a carrot on the end of the stick to look forward to. The ugly truth of this game is that there is absolutely no point at which you are rewarded for setting up your engine. You don't fight a special monster, you don't get to go into a cool new dungeon where they've been hiding the fun, you just keep grinding so you can keep grinding so you can keep grinding ad infinitum. Eventually, once I got my aforementioned build set up, the combat consisted of me holding A until everything died in every single fight. There was no variation, no thought, just me watching the endless waves of 6 enemies at a time dying to my machine between navigating the world's worst procgen dungeon maps. You could have achieved the same effect with a grey screen that lets you "hold A to  make numbers flash on the screen faster than you can read them!"

Other note: There are several different classes but they are set up so that they all build very slowly so you have to put multiple hours into a class to figure out whether you even like it or not. The good news is that they all result in the same numbers flashing across the screen (although sometimes with different font colors! How fun!) so you don't really have to worry that you're missing out because you chose a Cleric instead of a Hell Knight or whatever.

The game is a lot of really cool ideas poorly knit together that the community tries to ignore by abusing a handful of accidental overpowered cheese strategies. Consider it a warning when cheese strategies that other games would consider funny possibilities for repeat playthroughs or flat out patched out by devs are often the expected gameplay if you want to avoid 100+ level grinding. 

For example even in the early game, effects like mass curse will 1-shot your monsters while doing nothing to the enemy, unless you use a 1-shot cheese strat instead of playing the game.

Also, man. The writing sucks so bad. Between the really ugly UI, the low graphics, and the writing, its hard to stick around for so much obnoxious grinding and hard-wall luck-based mechanics to look for the gold nuggets. I could maybe ignore these issues if the gameplay was solid, but they only make the rough parts worse.

Difficult to interpret display, uninformative equipment decisions, and just plain boring.

The single biggest issue is the display. You're flying around in space, with hundreds of purely decorative small rocks in making a very crowded background, and it's very difficult to tell the difference between an actual rock you can pick or collide with and one of those background rocks. Only the larger rocks are obviously "real" (meaning they're actually on the same plane as your ship).

It doesn't help that it's a top-down display so you frequently can't see very far in front of your ship. Sometime there are rocks that are a collision hazard that you just can't see because they're off the edge of the map.

There are a ton of upgrades you can buy with your ship - but 90% of the time, it's not clear what they do. Why you'd want one drive over another, or one autopilot over another, for example.

I spent a couple of hours at it, and it was clear that the basic grind was pretty boring, doing the same stuff over and over. Find a rock, blast it, chase down the pieces, repeat.

STAY AWAY. It is frustrating and lazy combat game that ruins what could be a fun, realistic mining sim. It sells itself as "realistic science space mining" but fails to advertise the constant stream of NPC chatter and random attacks that can't be prevented. You are expected to run. Through an asteroid field in a mining ship that has realistic physics. You will drift and wobble before colliding with high speed ice boulders. If the random aggro encounters don't get you, the terrible ship handling and boulders will. This is a frustration engine.

I wanted to love this game and put in a lot of time to grind out the money to buy a good ship with the gear that makes mining not tedious. It took about 20 hours to get roughly 2M credits saved. Ships, crew, and equipment are very expensive and it will take about 1M for a starter ship. The 20 hours put into building a mining ship are wasted because now there people who just show up and attack me. Not pirates, they want nothing except instant chaos. The game stopped being fun when every 30 minutes a new ship sneaks up and start shooting my unarmed mining ship.

This is an sim-lite space combat game with invisible lasers and a constant parade of people in a part of space that should be empty. It has realistic science but unrealistic everything else - the economy is steady and uneventful, the weapons are boring, the NPCs useless, and the area is packed with people. It is simply not worth the time.

Having 59 available stats for each hero (=villager), 9 challenge types (fight is one of them) which use different combinations of stats for various real stats (like which stat is health, shield, offence, turn order), and late game expeditions with tens of villagers. Impossible to plan for anything except the fight, and even then you don't care much about each hero details, it's just too much to get through hundreds of stats in total. And the challenges/fight, while simple and boring, requires quite a bit of time to lay out villagers and enemies in order (auto seems to be good only against very weak opponents), use some abilities, and let the fight to resolve by itself. About the worst combat system I have seen which drags the game. The rest of the game is RPG-ish mixed with research & economic development, would had been pretty nice if not the combat.

The voice acting is bad to the point that I wish there just weren't any. There's just a lot of clicking in general, and I have no interest in playing for another 20 hours to see if MAYBE there is more to this game than meets the eye. Bad start.

I loved Stellaris, right up until it became clear I was being DLC farmed by Paradox while still waiting for them to fix major base-game issues. My experience went like this: love the game, can’t get enough of it, but can’t actually finish campaigns because of late-game performance problems. A new DLC releases, I buy it, the core issues remain unfixed. The DLC introduces new problems, those aren’t fixed either. Then another DLC releases. Rinse and repeat.

I may have been born in the morning, but I wasn’t born yesterday. There’s only so much rose-colored glasses can excuse, and I hit that limit a year ago. The only reason I hadn’t written a review sooner was I was hoping the performance issues would be fixed then out of pure disgust after they introduced the DLC subscription model.

So no, I do not recommend this game, not because it isn’t fun (it absolutely is), but because certain business practices shouldn’t be encouraged unless you’re comfortable being endlessly farmed for cash.

I would not recommend this title due to the developer/publisher's practices. The game is nearing 10 years old at this point, yet their only concern seems to be releasing overpriced DLC with only minor changes or content additions. The launcher and species editor are designed to highlight missing DLC, relying on FOMO to upsell more content. Updates regularly break community mods and old saves, leading many players suspect this is intentional. Some of the DLC packs where accused of being similar to existing mods that offered a better experience for free. 

The ultimate bundle currently sits at over $300. While game is fun and offers a great experience, the title is dragged down by Paradox's business practices. If you buy the game at base cost ($39.99), It would feel like playing a demo with many of the essential experiences paywalled. I don't think $300 is a reasonable price for the value provided.

The game is very fun, but is running into a large amount of performance issues that cant be overlooked. Modded or vanilla, after a certain point the game chokes to a hold even with a good CPU, GPU and ram because the game runs on a single thread one one CPU core. 

This bottle neck in the design of the engine means that each time the game releases an expansion performance takes a nose dive, if you want mods get ready for the game to slow to a crawl. This is because the game needs to run checks per tick per month for each pop in the game + other empire modifiers. Ever since 2.0 when the player base wanted "bigger number" the game engine simply cant handle it as it was never designed for it. 

Paradox would need to rewrite the engine to use multi threading and other optimization features at a structural level which would be excessively expensive.

This sucks because the game is a absolute blast,  and I love this game to my core, but cant bring myself to play anymore because the performance dive kills me, I dont want to sit there for 5+ minutes for a single month to pass at best, or have my game crash because it cant take the calculation (though that is more rare to be fair).

An ambitious paradox game that fails to model any systems with any depth and ultimately ends up as the shallowest grand strategy game. Despite that, it is still a fun experience, especially if you enjoy sci-fi. However the game has a deep identity problem and cannot figure out which systems it wants to model with how much depth.

Society & Politics is incredibly simplified to the point that ridiculous game play scenarios happen. For example, the game represents all ideology in the form of "ethics" which are a massively oversimplified collection of "the way the society is". Now this, in theory, could allow the player to customise their ideology much more than any other paradox game. Which sounds great! However, it seems the devs were worried that if they made this system too deep, the player would not want to bother. Therefore, there are very few of these "ethics" and they represent radically different ideologies. For example: There is the egalitarian ethic. Does this represent human rights? Does this represent democracy? Does this represent workers' rights? It seems it represents a bit of everything which compresses any political simulation.

Another funny thing is there is an ethic called "spiritualism". Which sort of makes sense, until you remember that empires that share ethics tend to like each other. This implies that a fanatic christian, for example, would like a fanatic muslim because they both like their god a lot?? Very stupid.

There is no demographics or any kind of depth to the economic simulation beyond inputs & outputs for your various buildings. Now, that's not necessarily bad but there is nothing really that the game does with any sort of depth.

Diplomacy & War is also incredibly simplified. Instead of "peace treaties" or "peace conferences" you get a casus belli by either claiming enemy systems or the game just giving you the subjugation casus belli if you are stronger than the enemy. Claiming enemy systems is just spending some currency to get the ability to get them after the war if you occupy them. There is no complex peace terms, reparations, negotiation etc. 

War has a bizarre timer that is "war exhaustion". Now, nearly every paradox game has a feature like this to prevent wars from going on for too long. However here, it is a timer that goes up regardless of whether you are winning or losing. It goes up somewhat faster if you are losing but even if you win every battle, your war exhaustion is going to be almost 1:1 with your enemy's. This means that every war has an arbitrary timer and if you can't beat the enemy within this period, the game forces you to end the war.

Also, on the depth again, occupation and war is incredibly simplified. When you occupy a system, you do not get to control anything. You don't get to even control the star base that you just took over, you do get to use it to repair your ships but that's about it. After occupying a system you'd expect the planets within the system to start starving or getting effected at all from the blockade but nope! Resources magically teleport to planets. Then you might think to bomb the planets but orbital bombardment is incredibly weak! This is especially bizarre because your ships can be equipped with nuclear bombs and yet you will have to bomb a planet for decades before they surrender. You will need to create a ground invasion army and manually invade every planet most of the time. This is very slow and very unrealistic but that's just how stellaris is in general.

In general, the game does not have any system or mechanic that is at all in depth. It tries to be a jack of all trades but feels lacklustre everywhere. You might still have fun but it's also equally likely to leave you frustrated.
