Stellaris is inspired by Master of Orion 2 and Star Trek.  The planets and star systems look great, but the space battles in Master of Orion 4 look better (just try cinematic mode!).  Stellaris doesn't feel like a space conquest then colonisation game, perhaps a good thing as too much of this can become boring in previous 4x strategy games.  In Stellaris you have to have a Casus Belli i.e. a legal reason for going to war.  Without one, you simply can’t declare war.  This may mean making a Claim on a star system.  

Unfortunately Stellaris feels more of a simulation than a game, but it does give players a number of stories as they play.

Stellaris has an in game encyclopaedia to explain in game concepts, and links to the Paradox wiki for more info.  Though the wiki's comprehensive, it could be more helpful at explaining how to play the game from a beginner's perspective and at giving playing and strategy tips, rather than focusing on tabulating information.  

The DLCs are very overpriced!    

I don't recommend Stellaris because it is too complicated, it's not enjoyable enough to be worth investing the time to fully learn the game.  It does have a basic tutorial (by explaining some concepts as you play), but often tries to teach you how to do things that you can't at the start of the game due to all the rules.  If you really want to buy it, play it on easiest difficulty without any DLCs activated at first as this will remove some of the complexity from the game.  That said the game is still too slow and longwinded (due to being rulebound) and complicated even without the DLCs and this isn't helped by the ongoing updates being made to the game as a lot of the Youtube tutorials are out of date.

JaxRayne has done some very helpful Youtube tutorials for beginners you can play along to.
Paradox's wiki's Beginners Guide is helpful, but needs updating https://stellaris.paradoxwikis.com/Beginner%27s_guide

I'd like to say that Stellaris is one of those games that with some refinement and new content is something I'd highly recommend, but the truth is, this game has been out for years with numerous DLC expansions and it still feels fundamentally broken.

I've sunk a good amount of time into this game (not as much as steam reports, but that's because it's been paused in the background for probably half that time), I digress, the point I'm trying to make is that I've played this game enough to say the designers hit on a nugget of fun and built all the wrong systems around it.

At the core of Stellaris allows for the ability to create a diverse and weird galaxy exploring species. Choosing the origin, government, traits, etc allows the player to developing their own vision of the species lore, backstory and headcannon, which inform all the decisions within the core gameplay loop of confronting and resolving the events and the emergent challenges unique to each playthrough, and it's amazing and is why I've come back exited and then ultimately dissapointed over and over again. 

The problem is that the designers have tried across numerous DLCs to expand on this ethos and most of the time it just results the game become more and more fiddly, and more and more complex. I'll wrap this up with a quick example; in my last play through I'd made it to mid-late game and was dealing with the crisis. Enemy fleets that were way tougher than I expected were rampaging through my system and killing the populations some of my planets. While I was making the tough decisions about where to send my fleets to try and have an impact while avoiding the main deathball of three 100k fleets of the crisis faction, I was constantly bombarded with irrelevant pop-ups demanding my attention. Pop-ups such as leader level-ups, excavation events, dialogs about the hacking I was attempting on another faction, and more. There's way too many systems competing for my attention and after awhile I just start mindlessly dismissing them which erodes the connection I have with the story the game is trying to build and the whole things devolves.

I have had mixed feelings about this game for years and mostly stuck around because sunken cost fallacy. 

The early game is fun as you explore the galaxy find new things and colonise systems and world. Midgame becomes meh were  marauders and some dlc try to keep things fresh with mixed results. End game tends to be tedious because of the amount of planets, stations and fleets you have to keep an eye on and in general there is no point sticking around after defeating the crisis and or fallen empires.

But the big problem is the paradox dlc policy. This sunken cost fallacy as mentioned before has made me stuck around for a long time. But the dlc does not feel worth it for the amount that you pay for it (usually between 10 and 20 euro). Even on sale I find the quality a bit sub-par. Early dlc added entirely new things such as the ascensions perks, megacorps, playing as gestalt empires and megastructures. Current dlc feels more like expensions for these earlier releases. That would have been acceptable were it not for the price tag. Some dlc does add new things but it seems to be very hit or miss. I have played with galactic storms and mostly did not register them unless they left behind a deposit on my planets.
What is very bad is that it seems that some bugs have not been resolved. Losing a game to the synth queen because the rest of the galaxy does not respond to her is not good. Having to manualy start attacking her because the trigger that should have started war with her did not work is even worse. The fact this happens with dlc released over a year ago is what for me broke the camals back.

Another sad side effect of all the dlc and updates is that mods keep being broken. Modmakers have to keep updating their work especially when paradox decides to overhaul the entire game economy or travel system. They have done this 3 times already.

In short the game has an fun start but starts to falter in mid/lategame. There have been attempts to solve this with dlc but the result are mixed and have created a rather pricy dlc policy. I wish I could be more positive but only pick this up if you have money and time to waste and don't mind the occasional jank.

I tried very hard, tried every combination of settings & mods to make this game feel's like a proper PDX game.
Took me some time, but I know realize this game is never gonna match my expectations, juste like every single PDX game since CKII.

This game is an arcade game, I don't expect arcade when playing a PDX product.
It seem's very huge & deep, but it's not.
Whatever your build is, you will 90% of the time play the exact same way, unity rush then win.

Empire strenght is almost only tied to unity, when reaching certain breakpoints your fleet power suddenly skyrocket, everybody becomes afraid of you & won't oppose any resistance.

All that's left after this point is the absurd spam from diplomacy, research, explorers & the war micro-fest.

This game is an RTS, not a 4X.

Stellaris is the kind of game that promises a galaxy and hands you a glorified spreadsheet. The early hours trick you with atmosphere and mystery, but the moment you start actually managing an empire, the illusion collapses. Everything becomes numbers, modifiers, and menus stacked on menus. It stops feeling like space and starts feeling like office work with starfields in the background.
The worst part is how much of the game is carved up into paid expansions. Every major system feels half‑finished until you buy another DLC, and even then it barely holds together. Instead of a grand strategy epic, you get a hollow framework constantly patched, reworked, and resold. The base game feels like a demo for the store page.
Stellaris isn’t a living galaxy. It’s a management chore wrapped in sci‑fi art, endlessly monetized and never truly complete. If you want a universe full of wonder, look elsewhere. If you want to micromanage job slots and resource trickles for hours, this is your game. For everyone else, it’s a galactic disappointment.

I've always hated Paradox and their disgusting DLC practices. I really, genuinely resent them for cornering as much of the 4X market as they have, because I think they make everything they touch worse.

But I also did the math on their "ten dollars a month for infinite DLC access you guys" subscription, because I've never seen that before. For the last 10 DLC releases, it's just under $170 of DLC released over 40 months. So you could pay $400 to get $170 worth of shit, and who knows how much of that shit is even any good? Paradox' shotgun spread approach to DLC means a bunch of it is ALREADY exploitative penny-pinching nonsense; now they're just going to flat-out ask you to give them money for nothing.

Fuck this company, and I will deliberately not purchase anything I see with their name on. I don't care they can write a game that engages a mysterious sixth human sense, cures cancer, and dispenses blowjobs; this company is *trash* and deserves to be regarded as such.

Feels like devs spend more time adding to the hundreds of dollars of DLC this game has, instead of fixing the core experience. The 4.0 update changes a TON, and it's not intuitive on how things work now (not that it was especially intuitive before this, but at least people got used to the quirks). So much stuff feels broken or locked behind DLC.

If you really want a paradox style 4x game in a sci-fi setting, then the base game is probably worth it (on sale).

One of my favourite strategy games that I cannot in good conscience recommend purchasing. 

Changing the balance of the entire game every few updates is their prerogative as the developers; while it deeply frustrates some players, it doesn't bother me so much. 

Adding more content every few months, even though the game is coming up on ten years old, is a gracious amount of care for the playerbase, and while some may find the number of different options to be bloating over time, I appreciate adding expanses of variety to one's expansion into the expanse of space.

But the entire game, with all that they've added, comes to more than $250 american dollars (if you buy the big bundle), and while three hours of playtime per dollar may be a good ratio to some, I have taken a while to get there, and I don't think I can advise anyone else to follow.

Extremely monotonous; even with all the variety of races every game plays out essentially the same.  Build up as fast as you can, build up a fleet as large as possible without bankrupting yourself, get absolutely wrecked at the end by ai "events" with fleets 20x larger than you can possibly build.

Complete waste of time.  Just go back to Master of Orion II if you have a space 4x itch to scratch.

Routine:

Pick-up stellaris for a few weeks to scratch that itch -> Win a few games -> lose a few more -> get a really fun long-term game going -> Paradox updates the game unannounced -> All saves broken due to mechanics being completely flipped on their head -> avoid like the plague for another year or 2 until I forget, yet again, that Paradox is like an toxic-ex that I keep going back to for more and more. 

I preordered this back in 2017, which is when you should have bought it. Don't buy this game now because it WILL break on you and it WILL ruin your immersion into the story of your save games, which has been one of Paradox's whole sticking points for their 4-x Grand Strategy games.

Stellaris used to be one of my favourite games and a lot of fun to play both with friends and alone with a bunch of mods. Ah, Paradox. At once purveyors of compelling “grand strategy” sims of almost unique ambition and complexity, and their own worst enemy. Shoveling expensive and maximalist DLCs out for years after release, alongside free updates which may or may not break the game, trash existing saves, and pile system upon spreadsheet-based system until the game resembles an accountant’s migraine, buying a Paradox title is always a gamble. Maybe it’ll work wonderfully, and reward your investment. Maybe it'll be unplayable – with or without some crucial DLC – now, or in the future.

I came to Paradox’s unique oeuvre by way of Europa Universalis IV, and mostly had a blast, even if I’d be considered a novice after sinking merely hundreds of hours into it. I also saw it devolve over the years I dipped in and out of it, as DLC proliferated and, if not all purchased, weird vestigial systems were bolted onto an already overwrought base game. So, I approached Stellaris for the first time in 2025 – nine years after its release in 2016 – with some trepidation. Where would Stellaris land on the Paradox roulette wheel?

In mechanical terms, Stellaris sits somewhere between the simulationist detail of EU IV and a classic 4X in the vein of Civilization. After choosing your starting species – the game will helpfully recommend which DLC to purchase to best enhance the experience, from the 23 options available at time of writing – you’ll set up shop in a corner of galaxy. From there, you’ll grow your empire from the lonely confines of your home system to galaxy-spanning empire. Before long, a clunky diplomacy system, including a time-consuming galactic council, will require you to juggle rivals’ opinions, infiltration levels and voting agendas. It can feel murky rather than Machiavellian. Fleets must be built and shuffled around your empire, though beyond this, there’s little need to worry about logistics. Minerals harvested on the other side of the galaxy can immediately be spent on building an apartment block in your new colony world. Constructing buildings and districts, and allocating different education levels of Pops to them, also demands time-consuming optimisation at higher difficulties. These duties can be turned over to an AI governor, but their decisions can be counterproductive. There’s also a ship editor, where your battle fleets can be customised, but it’s difficult to see what loudouts and tactics work, and why, in the brief pew-pew of fleet engagements. In essence, there are no shortage of gameplay options in Stellaris, but little guidance for the player about what’s important, or what the consequences of a decision might be. Enacting slavery, or ascending to psionic godhood, can feel a lot like reallocating your workforce from energy collectors to mining drills. It’s a lot of busywork, with relatively few interesting decisions to make.

Science fiction can be considered on a sliding scale of “hardness,” with the austerity and intellectual rigor of Arthur C. Clarke and Greg Egan at one end, and the “space fantasy” of Star Wars at the other. One might imagine that Stellaris, as a kind of sci-fi reimagining of Paradox’s Serious Historical Simulators, would join the likes of Alpha Centauri and SOMA at the harder end of the scale. Instead, it makes what seems a conscious effort to be breezy and engaging. Your civilisation starts the game with FTL, your homeworld politically-unified and at peace, and the colonisation of your home system well in hand, with a kind of “no biggie” insouciance. This is not a criticism, as such, but a warning that players looking to recreate The Expanse, or Kim Stanley Robinson’s Mars trilogy, may find their mileage will vary. Stellaris is perhaps tonally closest to Star Trek. Though, if I’m honest, the frequent snippets and micro-narratives would seem most at home in a lesser iteration of that franchise, without the characterisation or sociological insight that elevated the likes of The Next Generation. 

Artistically, Stellaris elides much of the wonder and terror that might come with the inhuman scales of galaxy-spanning empire for cheery accessibility. Solar systems sit in neat, static astrolabes. In the course of a few in-game years – and less real-time than it might take you to pop to the shops for milk – you’ll have surveyed your first neighboring star system, colonized an alien world, and made first contact with aliens. Later additions – Dyson spheres! Star-killing superweapons! – land with a similar uncanny weightlessness. There’s a whiff of box-ticking to some of the features in Stellaris, less about adding mechanical or thematic depth, and more about the designers ensuring every science fiction trope is represented. As in EU IV, some fine incidental sound design helps build atmosphere, but uncanny character portraits and a clutter of icons detract from it. A sub-Vangelis score burbles away in the background: Paradox still desperately need an option to introduce periods of silence between their music tracks.

For all that Europa Universalis looked and played like a world map stapled to fifty Excel spreadsheets, I happily sunk several hundred hours into it, and probably would have kept going had the DLC arms race not rendered the game unplayable. The genius of Paradox’s detailed simulations is in allowing hugely compelling, player-driven stories to emerge from the mechanical murk. In EU IV, the early modern era unfolds over 50-hour campaigns, with the British colonisation of the Americas, France’s wars of conquest in Europe, and the rise and fall of the Spanish empire all arising organically from the game’s systems. Crucially, the player could change the course of history; conquer Spain, as Morocco, say, and leave the Incas to flourish unmolested, or march west from Korea to central Asia, and squash Muscovy before they become the Russian Empire. The simulation would react in fascinating and unpredictable ways. By pureeing down all the quirks and specifics of world history, and extruding it as a sci-fi paste, Stellaris is a less gripping sandbox. The game still offers a rich playground, and gives you commendable freedom to roleplay as alien religious zealots, an AI hivemind, or ultra-capitalist jellyfish. But for me, at least, vanquishing the Ryzongian Hierophants of the Inner Spiral Arm was not as evocative as unifying Italy early and wrestling Napoleonic France to hard-fought defeat. The “crisis” served up toward the endgame is a welcome attempt to reintroduce dynamism and peril during a period many 4X games struggle with. But in arriving from nowhere and almost always taking the form of an invading horde, it again misses an opportunity for gameplay or thematic complexity. What if the player’s actions in the early game came back to haunt them in the endgame crisis? What if difficult decisions had to be made about how best to respond – not just throwing fleets at the invading horde until they’re deleted – and what if Stellaris engaged with those decisions with any seriousness? Works like Iain M. Banks’ Excession have shown how a galaxy-spanning civilisation can be thrown into turmoil by the unexpected, but there’s nothing so subtle to be found here. 

Where, then, does Stellaris land on the Paradox roulette wheel? At time of writing, I found it to be mediocre. A more traditional 4X than its grand strategy predecessors, with many of the rough edges sanded off, and offering some mechanical depth as well as breadth. It’s strongest, for me, during the early game, when suffused with a kind of optimistic, exploratory wonder. Both in terms of gameplay and performance, Stellaris becomes a slug-fest in the late game. After completing one campaign, I felt that I’d seen most of what Stellaris had to offer. If you’re new to Paradox, Crusader Kings III probably exemplifies the best of the studio. Otherwise, Sid Meier’s Alpha Centauri remains a fine 4X with genre-leading writing and worldbuilding.

Suffice it to say that this game is no longer the game I originally paid for. Stellaris has gone through several interations early on, most of which drastically (for the good) changed the overall direction of the game. However with the last major change made a while back (changes to the way planet management and increase in the number of materials (ie addition of things like alloys) I feel the game has become obfuscated for the sake of obfuscation or in order to just prolong it.

You need to build complex networks to get higher grade materials which is made extremely difficult if you didnt not already build your networks with the required balancing of the higher grade materials already in mind. Admittedly this is probably the intent, to give you something to do in the midgame, or something to allow people to min-max early on, for me it feels like many pathways were just closed off and now there are very strict design pathways that need to be followed. This was also evident in other changes like ship balancing, before it felt there were several optimal fleet setups now it feels you always need to have a very balanced loadout. This may be a change for the good in some scenarios like earlier it was possible to just spam torpedo frigates and could easily dominate the AI, this no longer the case, not because AI now builds flak against small ships but rather that the small ships are just useless against larger ones in late game. This definitely improved performance especially in multiplayer where such small ship spam could easily lead to desyncs, I still feel like in a game that appeals to your limitless opportunities, it feels like the game punishes you for not following strict pathways. 

The game has also in typical paradox fashion ben EU4'ified, in that you need a handful of DLCs to actually play the intended full game, otherwise its like playing monopoly without half of the properties.

TLDR Game is complicated and does not feel like it has nice progression, many mechanics are just do this thing because it exists (like panetary management). Roleplay is harder because optimal paths seem way better than all others and if you intend to make it to late game you best follow this strict pathways

It makes me so incredibly sad to tick the thumbs down for this game. 

For a long, long time, Stellaris was my favourite 4x of all time. The initial release was shaky but had good bones, bones which were reinforced and built upon with some fantastic updates and DLC over the years. The aesthetic was wonderful, the musical score was wonderful, the sheer detail that has been added to allow customisation of your civilisation is incredible, and the variety of events and crises you could encounter meant no two games ever played the same.

But we've long since passed the point where the game should have been called done, and any further changes left for a sequel. We're now into the 3rd round of "patches" which effectively remake core systems of the game from the ground up in order to make it work with the new DLC they keep adding.

I can't say this is my favourite 4x anymore becuase it isn't even the same game that I bought in 2016 anymore. Nor is it the same game I sunk hundreds of hours into during the sweet spot during late patch 2.0 and early patch 3.0. Now we're well into 4.0 and the game is increasingly bloated, broken, and unrecognisable.

So many of these massive, sweeping changes to the core game should have been saved for Stellaris 2, but instead it seems that either good intentions have paved the way to hell, or Paradox simply did their thing and pushed for more DLC and more monetisation, as they always do, and turned the game into a live service mess which it was never designed to be.

Stellaris is one of my favourite games, that I absolutely cannot recommend.
Strarting with the good:
- One of the best Grand Strategy/4X games out there, you can get lost in all the strategy and empire building if that's what you're into, and that's one of the best things about this game
- Great replayability, especially with mods
- Speaking of which, mods. Mods are the absolute CORE of this game for me, things like Gigastructures, ACOT, extended traditions, more planet mods etc. add like 300% of content into Stellaris, which to be fair made the base game simply boring after playing with many of those great mods for so long, which brings me to the bad about the game unfortunatelly

The bad:
- Right now as of stellaris 4.0 and higher the game has changed completely, it seems like the devs just make many core feature of Stellaris from the ground up instead of just updating the game, which for me makes the game unrecognizable, bloated, and forces you to learn basic mechanincs all over again with each patch, which with a game like that takes a looot of time, it's pretty sad in my opinion, it's basically looking at something you love slowly becoming unrecognizable, but each to their own I guess. This also presents a huge issue which is if you want to play on a rolled back version, most great mods are not supported or their legacy versions just don't work, which makes the base game just boring, and the newer versions on the other hand lack many mods that are just broken post updates, because the dev team doesn't care about that either
- 400 $ in DLC is a joke, when you buy the base game you have like 50% of mechanics blocked behind DLCs, and while the new updates seemingly have "free" components, they are made with paid content in mind, which would be reasonable if Paradox wasn't simply greedy with it, this is much more than just making some money off a game that's out for some time it's basically a subscription model for a singleplayer game, and the quantity is disgusting and not at all reflected in quality

Should you buy this game? If you have too much money to spend and don't value your time when your favourite mechanics and mods will inevitably get broken, then sure, otherwise don't do this to yourself, it's sad what became of this great game.

I've enjoyed this game for a long time, but I agree with other reviews saying it should have been called done a long time ago. While the new systems seem okay from the time I have spent with them, it is telling that they feel the need to fully rework the game every few months. Now, I might have overlooked that for a bit longer, but the inclusion of generative AI in any point in development, let alone in the conceptualization of new elements and voice acting, really just serves as a death knell for this game. It demonstrates a supreme laziness in its continued development which is now doomed to only grow worse. If they can't commission a concept artist or have a VA for a few advisor lines, then how can the quality or consistency of anything from here on out be trusted?

In short, a year ago I would have gladly recommended this game. Now, it is becoming little more than a corpse puppeted around by the mediocrity machine. I cannot in good conscience encourage anyone to pick it up at this point.

Stellaris is probably one of my all-time favorite games, but the 4.0 update has really wrecked the game in many aspects. Not that it was perfect before, but it's really at an all-time low.

- Performance - which was 4.0's main promise - is significantly worse than before.
- UI is in the gutter: some of the new screens are plain bad, others are bugged.
- Game is very unstable, prone to crashes, or desyncs in multiplayer, while in my experience it was very stable before.
- The game is missing many QoL features, UX is very bloated. 
- The AI just plain stops working after enough time passes and entirely relies on cheats for any semblance of competence. This creates an unmissable imbalance between the AI being inexplicably strong early game, and inept late game. You need to carefully engineer your game settings between Difficulty, Scaling Difficulty, Difficulty Adjusted AI Modifiers, and the predicted strenght of your own build to have a decent game.
- There is major power creep, little balance to speak of, and old content is never brought up to the standard of the latest updates (when are Necrophages getting fixed?)

These are all well known issues, widely talked about in the community, but Paradox continues to favor releasing new paid content over fixing the game, as they have already ***RELEASED 3 DLCS SINCE 4.0***, leaving a large amount of major issues unaddressed.
 
This one might be personal preference but the main practical change of 4.0, the new districts/buildings system, is really not doing it for me. Especially, stacking special building and their modifiers on a planet or two is *fun*. But now with all the buildings you are required to have on any given planets, and with the reduced total slots numbers, you rarely get the ever have more than one or two unique buil

Not 4.0 related but the portraits they've been adding for a couple years now are for the most part rubbish. The only good batch was the Mammals one that came with BioGenesis. I would not mind if there was a way to disable portraits but there isn't, so you have to deal with the galaxy being filled with low quality meat puppets.

Lastly, I think a lot of people might not relate to all the issues I have, especially people with fewer hours spent on the game. People will still love the game and play it, as will I, because it is great. But to me it is really saddening that even though the game is supported with patches and paid content, Paradox is somehow not making it objectively better over time. What are we paying money for?

TLDR; This is a DLC milking farm, while core problems like end game performance remain a big problem.

Paradox seems more focused to pop out new shiny (and expensive) DLC than to fix existing problems and integration of previously added systems(by DLC or Free-patch). Not to mention the balance issues that rise from new systems/units/technologies.
I honestly think they could succeed fixing most problems if they just focused on those, instead of pushing DLCs.

This game went from one of my favorite games to one of the absolutely worst, most broken, most frustrating experiences of all time. Let me fire off a few random bugs caused by broken mechanics that have been redone so many times there's no hope of even identifying what mechanic or system is causing the issues in the first place.

1) Did you know that a player Radical Purifier (ie a species type that CANNOT DO ANY DIPLOMACY) has to manually shut off border access after a truce? I didn't know that. A friend and I were playing a campaign, both of us were purifiers, we were back to back, and because they had a truce and didn't close the borders after the truce was over the enemy fleets were able to waltz right through their territory and into my backlines. That was fun to discover when i'm having to manage a war that's suddenly being waged on 3 fronts.

2) Did you know that if a horde successor state joins a federation, enters into a federation war, and when you invade said horde successor state and have armies on their planets that if the horde successor state suddenly turns neutral and drops from the federation your armies will remain STUCK ON THE PLANET? I didn't know that. I was invading multiple planets at once in a blitz. I lost all of my ground armies.

3) Did you know that if a planetary FTL inhibitor is researched during a war when your fleet is in an enemy system with an inhabited planet, the ftl inhibitor will KEEP YOUR FLEET FROM GOING ANYWHERE?!? Yeah. That was fun to find out when my 200k fleet was suddenly stuck on in a system while the enemy fleets ran AROUND that system.

4) Did you know that if a pre-ftl species spawns from an event in your home system and you aren't careful, they will STEAL YOUR HOME SYSTEM? YEAH. THAT WAS FUN. SUCH A GREAT MECHANIC. IM SURE MY VICIOUSLY XENOPHOBIC EMPIRE WOULD GIVE OUR HOME SYSTEM AWAY TO SOME SENTIENT ROCKS THAT WERE LIVING UNDER THE SURFACE OF OUR MOON. THAT MAKES SO MUCH SENSE.

5) Did you know that it doesn't matter what your carefully planned starbases and planets setups are, the AI will decide to just throw it all away and build 5 luxury buildings? Yep. Saw that repeatedly. Luxury buildings. WHY. WHY ARE THEY EVEN A THING.

6) Did you know that starbase defenses are basically worthless by midgame? Yeah. They just are. They aren't even good speedbumps. There's no point to building any defenses, any defense platforms, nothing, they just die. The starbases haven't been touched in years despite every other system of the game being completely rewritten from the ground up. As such, they've been powercrept so horrifically that they're completely and utterly worthless as anything except trash cans to throw your alloys away in.

This game is dumb, this game will make you rage, and if you make it through all that, you will have to 1v1 whatever endgame crisis an idiot ai will trigger and then none of the AIs will effectively fight, or worst, will just roll over and die to and make the endgame crisis stronger.

The disgusting and appalling game maintenance completely ruins the game experience for all players and achieves its so-called challenge and "complete reckoning" (this term is directly taken from the update announcement) by mindlessly filling enemy stats with more zeros. Through bottomless numerical modifications, it equally drags all players, regardless of experience or skill, to the same low level, making one wonder if this is the level of the maintenance staff themselves. There is no reasonable way to finish a game in 10 hours, but did the game's publisher notice that the game becomes drastically laggy after 10 hours? Can you see that a large amount of poor programming is actually straining device performance? While forcing players to finish the game quickly with non-existent performance optimizations, they extend the game time per match through outrageous and meaningless delays, and declare players "too powerful," drastically reducing the resources available to all players and increasing costs (because they shamelessly claim this has special significance), making it impossible for players to even pay for their own expenses with their own income. Even worse, there's the ridiculous claim that "you can build a maximum of 0.4 dreadnoughts." If the maintenance team persists, I will have to point out that the game's pricing is "numerically excessive" and be forced to recommend that all players "optimize" it out of their shopping carts.

The game publisher has repeatedly announced that it will "liquidate" its players, and in fact, it has done just that—every update severely punishes players with bottomless numerical reductions and deleting content. As players, we call each update a "half-price cut," and the recent update we'd rather call an "amputation, but from the hairline."

Furthermore, the game publisher makes hasty modifications to its own game's economic system without any understanding of it. After each update, a large amount of content becomes abnormal and is omitted, with the publisher claiming to have overlooked something, but in reality, it's a case of overlooking something. Shouldn't they have any reflection? Actually, no; they just announce they will consider it and then disappear without a trace.

TL:DR - Low replayability, it's not a 4x, it's a bad rp game, lategame is tedious, bad tutorial.

Please note that most of my hours on this game are leaving it on while I am sleeping or at work, I probably have a few dozen real hours.

I'm generally a fan of Paradox games, and there are times where I have enjoyed playing this game, but ultimately I can't recommend it. It's definitely not a HORRIBLE game by any measure, but ultimately I don't think it's worth a large amount of your time or money.

The main flaws of this game is as follows:

1. Lack of Replayability - There simply isn't a lot of meaningful variety across different playthroughs. Take the maps for instance. In a game like Civilization 5 you might play on a Pangea map one playthrough, then a small continents playthrough next time, then an archipelago playthrough the match after that. And even if you played the same civ with the same playstyle, the maps would significantly change the feeling of the game. Here, there is some variety in the map, but there is only so much you can do with outer space, so maps never feel signifacntly different in interesting ways.

The second part that really hurts replayability is that in typical Paradox fashion it is just a blobbing game. To bring up Civilization 5 again, in that game there isn't only conquest victory. There are also things like a peaceful science victory, or a diplomatic victory. The way you win in this game is by getting a large score, which you do by blobbing. Science and diplomacy can help, but at its core this is still a blobbing game, and each playthrough will largely feel similar due to that.

The third part that hurts replayability is the lack of meaningfully different traits. You can make your own custom country where you can say, give them militarism for some extra military bonuses, but it is just a small modifier, and a playthrough where you go spiritualism for a unity bonus doesn't feel that different than going materialism for a robot upkeep bonus. There are SOME traits that spice things up a bit, like fanatic purifier is better at war but can't do diplomacy, or a hivemind doesn't need consumer goods, but these sorts of traits are usually all about REMOVING something from you that you can usually do, so it isn't that interesting or different. There are still some things you can do to spice up your faction when creating them in Stellaris, but at the end of the day I would say you only get maybe 7 good distinct playthroughs or so from that, which is not enough.


2. You buy this game thinking it is a 4x game, but really it is a bad roleplaying game - What I mean by that is if there is no replayability, then what is the appeal of the game? A lot of people who like this game like it for the roleplay aspect. Like I can try and make the Covenant from Halo, or I can try and make the humans from Star Trek. But I think this appeal falls flat because the game doesn't give you a lot to roleplay with, you have to make up your context yourself. There are origins which help a bit, but ultimately if I am roleplaying as the covenant from Halo I give myself maybe the spiritual and militarist traits and that is that, just some small passive buffs. The game is not giving you a lot to work with. 

Compare that to Europa Universalis 4. In that game you play as real historical countries, so you can have those existing cultures to give you an expansive context for your roleplaying. For instance if you are playing as America you have all of American culture to help you imagine how the country you are playing is like, instead of just a few traits you can select from a list. And on top of that you have historical or ahistorical goals to enhance your roleplaying. I get much more immersed conquering the Western Hemisphere as the Inca as compared to conquering some space-faring hivemind as the replicators from Stargate. In the latter you blob just to blob, because that is what Stellaris is about.

3. Lategame is tedious. This is a game where you have to micromanage and it won't be a problem early game, but once you become significantly large and have many planets you will have too much to manage and the entire experience is insufferable. 

There are some good points as well to the game. It is easy to learn for a Paradox game, although not from the tutorial. The tutorial is trash, you need to look up on forums to learn how to play. The discovery and writing is good. (Although you will quickly run into the same events over and over.) The combat is not as tedious as some other 4x games. You can just doomstack. Bombarding planets from orbit is very pretty and quite the power trip.

Tried very hard to enjoy this game since it released on steam. I thought if I waited almost a decade and try again when it was more refined I would find it fun, but that wasn't the case. 
Pros: 
It's okay for the first game you play.
The way multi-species population is handles is mildly interesting.
Having established AI species (termed Fallen Empires) present in the campaigns is a neat way to restrict blind rushing early aggression.
Cons: 
Very low replayability even when playing as the most unique species you can create. Whether you are a friendly diplomatic species of tolerance or a evil hivemind to consume the galaxy you will find yourself doing 80% of the same things every game. The only impactful difference between campaigns I found were how aggressive you are to the other species and the end game scenarios. It's odd as Paradox usually really does well with replayability on their other titles, but seemed to have dropped the ball for Stellaris.
Awful UI and controls. There are entire menu panels that only have a single button that could of been consolidated into other menus. 
The DLCs are VERY overpriced and add LITTLE content to the game. I tried 3 of them out and none of them felt they were worth it, even when they were on sale for half off. 
Notification spam is incredibly annoying, I don't think I need a visual and audio alert when my unit enters a battle with a unit I told it to fight. I think I'm well aware that was going to happen when I issued the order. I don't think i need icons popping up at the top of my screen to remind me about the main screen pop-up the game just shoved in my face.

While I did not enjoy this game, steamchart shows Stellaris has maintained quite a dedicated player base throughout the past decade so I may be an outlier. I would only recommend Stellaris if you already bored of Paradox's other titles such EU, HoI, and CK. Otherwise I would recommend any of those titles well over Stellaris.

TL;DR: Plains of Pain has solid survival fundamentals and can be fun if you’re tolerant of bugs and rough edges. Don’t buy it unless you’re willing to deal with frequent glitches and missing quality of life features while it’s still a work in progress.

At first glance, Plains of Pain feels like a solid survival game with decent mechanics and real potential. Unfortunately, there are several foundational problems that need to be addressed before it can be considered good.

A lot of the writing feels AI assisted, including forced, AI style jokes that don’t land. Much of the artwork also appears AI generated. The core gameplay systems don’t help either. Crafting, searching, and inventory management feel lifted straight from the mobile game Last Day on Earth. The inventory is especially bad, completely disorganized, with basic quality of life features like sorting missing.

Some mechanics are just outright terrible. For example, you can accidentally drink gasoline. When you find a barrel, the game gives you two options… drink first, then check what it is. That shouldn’t even be possible. No survival game should allow a player to casually drink gasoline by mistake. It’s just poor design.

This isn’t even getting into the technical issues. The game is full of glitches… falling through the map, vehicles clipping out of existence, physics completely breaking. The game already makes everything a struggle by design, so when you finally build a vehicle and it disappears into the terrain or launches itself because it hit a small bump wrong, that’s a serious problem.

That said, if you’re someone who’s tolerant of bugs, glitches, and rough edges, there is fun to be found here. As a survival game, Plains of Pain can be enjoyable, and its core ideas show promise. This is clearly a work in progress, and future updates could significantly improve the experience.

Just be cautious. Don’t buy this game unless you’re willing to push through frequent bugs, frustrating design decisions, and missing quality of life features. If you are, you may still find it worth exploring. If not, it’s better to wait and see how it develops.

I want to like it, but it isn't good.

Yet.

The pitch is a compelling one - a survival game where you start from nothing to become a real player in the wastes, a competitor to Kenshi that isn't an RTS poorly bashed into the shape of an open-world game. It's a promising premise, and one that's been attempted plenty of times, but succeeded with nearly not at all.
This one's path to power is trading, like (most iconically) Elite, the much more obscure Caravaneer, or Kenshi itself.

There's a vehicle repair system (I hesitate to say 'construction' because you're just sticking parts back on derelicts) that works and can send you on fun-or-tedious fetch quests back and forth across the wastes, but as a gearhead I'm vaguely annoyed that all the vehicles present, even those based on real examples, are hovercars.

The devs seem to want this game to be better, but right now it's very early in development. 

Among its issues are:
-Gathering (mining, salvaging, lumbering, etc) is deeply tedious, requiring multiple button presses to finish a given resource node. One player actively recommends the use of AutoHotKey to end-run what should be a basic QoL feature.
-Multiple traders will have the exact same tradegoods and requests, which wouldn't be an issue if one of the late-game trading quests (usually Gun Parts) didn't *consistently* spawn a buyer but no sellers. 
-The tutorial quest can be almost effortlessly broken, and plot-relevant non-tradeable items spawn consistently in high-level lootables. 
-One high-tier food has an ingredient that only spawns at one trader, and sometimes not at all. 
-Dialogue with NPCs is progressed by looking at them, looking away, and looking at them again.

There's the bones of a solid game here, but that's all they are at this point: bones. If you're willing to gamble on a bare chassis and a pile of scrap metal, then have at it, but I'll give this another few updates to mature before I try it again.

I don’t understand how people can still put up with this anymore. No hard feelings toward the developer—I know he’s doing his best—but V008 just came out and the game is still completely broken.

First, the AI at night either sees nothing at all or can see perfectly in complete darkness. You can be one meter away from them on max difficulty inside a house and they won’t notice you, but they’ll spot you from 50 meters away in total darkness on the new Kabul map.

Moreover, the new maps look good from a design standpoint, but they are completely unplayable solo. Even when playing in a squad, I don’t understand how no one complains. All the AI stacks in the middle of open areas in one single spot, which is one of the most ridiculous things I’ve ever seen. The fact that the game is hard is great—and it should be—but having to throw four grenades just to kill them all (because the new suppressing fire mechanic makes it impossible to see anything) feels extremely unrealistic.

The game claims to be a good representation of what milsim players want, but in reality it falls far short of what a game should be. The AI is so bad that it doesn’t even make sense to play solo or in a squad, since the same problems occur. There are barely any PvP servers most of the time, and the game is also full of small bugs that make it very annoying to play.

I’m a big believer in small developers doing their best to create the kind of milsim games that big companies won’t offer, but this just feels like a weapon-showcase fantasy.

I really wanted to like Inside the Backrooms, but the experience ended up being frustrating rather than frightening. The level design felt messy and uncreative—each area was more annoying than immersive, and instead of atmosphere, I mostly got confusion and irritation.

The monsters were a major problem. There were far too many of them, and instead of building tension, they just turned the game into a nonstop chase simulator. Their behavior wasn’t scary, just relentless and aggravating, making every section feel like a chore rather than a challenge.

And despite all of that, the game somehow still wasn’t scary. The scares never landed, the ambience fell flat, and the whole thing lacked the creeping dread the Backrooms concept is supposed to deliver.

The final cave level was the worst of all—absolutely brutal to get through, but not in a satisfying way. It felt more like punishment than difficulty, dragging on and killing any remaining enjoyment.

Overall, Inside the Backrooms completely missed the mark for me. Instead of fear or fun, I was left with frustration. Not recommended.

Really need a "neutral" option here. The soundtrack called "カタシロと願い" which appears repeatedly in all sorts of important scenes is pretty good. It alone evoked more emotion than most of the scene text. I do actually like the overall idea and even the structure, and the story does play some interesting games with narratives. There are several times I needed to re-contextualize a scene, then re-contextualize again, then again. I like that. The overall execution left quite a lot to be desired though.

The transition between slice-of-life and horror and romance (?) and rural revitalization high school club activity (??) is quite awkward. It's the feeling that even if most things are indeed properly foreshadowed, we were still switching between serious plots and tropey slice-of-life comedy way too quickly. The writing is a bit unpolished, but fine for the journey. The jumping back-and-forth was probably partially necessary for the narrative to work, but is definitely a bit disorienting. The pacing looks more like a musical theater rather than visual novel.

3 hrs in, 0 engagement. I understand that we need a buildup before the "actual plot", but I ain't patient enough to deal with those insanely boring texts. I was hoping for Atri 2.0 but this is not even close.

Storyline has merit but the writing is not great. Especially the H-scenes need someone with writing flair and not just copying the subtitles of an overdone porno.

Overall can't really find interest in game anymore, there was no meaningful addition from the predecessors. Hoping for better quality -content- in future games.

[h1] I enjoy this game, but still don't recommend it [/h1]

This game is a very creative idea, executed very well in practise
It's a little hard on lower-end machines, and has a couple issues with loading-times, but for a free game, it's very good.

[h2] However, the problem lies in the community and it's moderation [/h2]

A large portion of this game is filled with unsavoury individuals, a lot of racial, homophobic, transphobic or otherwiser-similar types of language i've found to be commonplace.
Additionally, a surpising amount of outfits people use on this game (particularly around rooms centred around the LGBTQIA+, Furry, or any other niche-interest commonly associated with queer folk) are blatently in reference to Adolf Hitler, giving the classic "no-no salute" quite often.

Admittedly, the report-system is easy to use, quite broad in options, and votekicking is quite lax.
But, if you opt for reporting as opposed to votekicking (votekicking having it's own set of flaws when large groups of these people band together), you usually need to wait upwards of several days to get acknowledgement if your report went through.

Additionally, this game uses AI voice-moderation, which in my time playing this game works... terribly.
People have been freely allowed to say hateful rhetoric en-masse in the game with no punishment, the longest I have witnessed was a man sexually harassing and hurling racial slurs at what sounded like (to me) an underage child and his boyfriend for over 5 hours without punishment.
Meanwhile, I have been muted for something harmless, like talking about my favourite cartoon or some food I cooked.

Or at least that's what it seems like.
[h2] There is a crucial flaw in how Rec Room's AI Voice-Moderation works. [/h2]

Something I have noticed in my time playing is that the AI Automod seems to specifically work in such a way so that it punishes you without allowing you to improve behaviour.

For example, if you broke the rules, lets say you got in an argument and told the other person to kill themself, (which you shouldn't say); you would either get muted immediately, which is the good end-result.
OR, you may be allowed "off the hook" for it; until several hours - or even days - later, where you will get muted seemingly without reason at the time.

The Automod can mute people even a week later than when the incident occured; meaning that (to the user), it doesn't matter what they say or when they say it, because it effectively would feel random.
I will give a personal example.

in a public room, i tested with a friend of mine.
I called her the F-slur (which she AND I can both reclaim), and then I muted my microphone.
In-game, I did not type or use my microphone for 3 days, and then after 60~ hours, I suddenly recieved an account warning.

To any other player, this would feel like a mute (or possibly a ban) without reason; as they would of completely forgotten about what they did by the time punishment came.
This encouraged players to stop caring about the threat of moderation, as "it doesn't matter what I do becuase it's random anyway."

[h2] Aside from this issue... [/h2]

Rec Room has great technical usage, it's a great game to play with your friends in private Rooms, or in parties together; but it appears to me that it's completely ruined by two things: It's community and it's AI Voice Moderation

[h1] Great game ruined by bad people and bad moderation; 4/10 [/h1]

All I have to say is, this game died not because of it getting boring or lack of content like the devs think. This game died because when the developers of the game were told by the entire community not to do something, they did it anyways. The very first instance of this happening, is when the developers switched from the 3D watch menu to the 2D watch menu. Everyone loathed the change, and the developers even said on a youtube video (ama I believe) that they do not care that the community wants 3D UI. They pushed for the 2D UI simply because it would allow them to expand specific features.
Later in the years of rec room, weekly challenges were being repeated, and eventually they were limited to monthly challenges. Rec Room has made their argument clear on its removal; however, plenty of other youtubers and community representatives have made equally compelling counter arguments.
And again later in the years, rec room has begun shifting its focus on UGC creation and its token economy (over charging items).
And again again later in the years, rec room begins making a push to remove the original bean body. They claimed it will improve performance in the game due to better culling and rendering; however, present day any RRO I play, has had far lower fps then ever. Hard Example: I could get 144fps (on Index) in Dodgeball with a full lobby back in 2020-2022, BUT NOW, I can only get consistent frames of 80-90fps (still on Index) forcing me to lower my target hz.

Anyways, not going to even talk about how bad their idea is with this AI slop, but yeeaahhhhh.... this game is dead.

As a veteran player of 2018 (13th of December i signed up)

I think this game had everything and lost it all.

"Golden era" 2018-2022 (As a lot of veteran player's describe it including myself)
The golden era of rec room was the era where rec room was constantly improving and had a non to minimum toxic community, the games were good, the lobby's were social, it was ORIGINAL. That is what made rec room stand out, it's originality and community, Game's were super creative, costumes were easy to play for, not grind.
Overall what rec room put on paper, seemed to be true. it was great.

"Fall of rec room" 2022-2025
In late 2022 rec room prices for tokens became higher and higher, it was dealable with for sure but was disappointing, then moderation got seemly worse and worse, more toxic player's would join lobby's to purposely ruin them, only vote-kicking was our saviour.

As the year's continued prices became incredibly high, AI slop took over artist's work. Player base continued to become toxic and younger, moderation became horrible, Toddler's would scream down the microphone in lobby's, people would be harassed for being "broke" to being a different skin colour.

The game obviously started to fall apart entirely. As the game got worse and worse, the originality that once was housed in rec room, dispersed into nothingness. The developer's would try to impress investor's rather than listen and support the community they built.

Eventually rec room's idea's got too big for it's head and they ended up firing a lot of their staff. The company who made rec room, (Against Gravity Corp) would hit US $3.5 billion.

They made the mark on their economy at the expense of their player base. It no longer has the spark it once promised to hold for the community.

I do not recommend this game to anyone.

Call me blinded by nostalgia, but something about the way the game feels, looks, and plays is very... corporate now.
This is because they are doing everything they can to bring investors in.
Consistently throughout these past few months, AI has been crammed into several areas of this game (as AI has been in everything), taking away one of the most encouraged ideas of this game - that being creativity.
It has been so hurtful to see such a wonderful game become worse and worse over time, as it fills with updates that nobody asks for (other than people investing) and slowly any player that isn't a child is leaving.
It is an unfortunate circumstance for what was once an amazing game.
Maybe one day I will be able to edit this and say I was wrong, but for now my point stands.
I don't recommend this game.

LONG VERSION:

Don't play this game anymore.

Rec Room is a mess of a game and it shows. The game is a mash between something original and being a Roblox clone because the game's direction has been shifted a million different times. Rec Room also just generally runs terrible on anything that hasn't released in the past 2 years. Many of these issues didn't exist when I first joined on Quest 2 in 2021, but it seems that the only direction this game is going is downhill, which sucks. I guess all good things gotta get blasted huh. If I was writing this review when I first joined, I would strongly recommend it, but in it's current state I would just stay away from it. It's impossible for me to convey my opinions about every little thing they've done to this game in this review, but just know that they've mismanaged the hell out of it and removed almost everything good about it. There's some great video essay/documentary/retrospectives about the issues with this game, and I recommend you watch those to get the full picture.

Rec Room in 2019-2023 was an amazing game that featured awesome social interactions, rec room originals, a good orientation system, creative people/community, and exciting updates. The game had much to offer in terms of community, building, and content I have 707 hours on steam and over 1,300 hours on PlayStation, this game was extremely fun and engaging. However mid 2023 to the present day I cannot play this game anymore for various reasons new and old:

-	Poor optimization
-	Anti-consumer practices
-	Community decline
-	The push for A.I
-	Push for user generated content over rec room originals
-	Lack of good updates for existing rec room originals as well as lack of new rec room originals.
-	Price inflation for items 
-	Free items now costing money for new players
-	Removal of PSVR 1 support
-	Removal of Quest 1 support
-	Laying off over 50% of their staff
-	Loss of identity, it used to have a really cool college theme that was unique, now it is trying to be a ROBLOX clone
-	Prioritizing user made items instead of original made items
-	Pushing A.I slope user-made games
-	Full body avatar, this could have been a really cool addition if they actually utilized it with full body tracking and if they didn’t change the art style of the in-game avatars
-	Removal of the old VR UI (it was 1000 times better than the one they have now)
-	Removal of the OG orientation system
-	Prioritizing screen players over VR players
-	Volunteer moderation (one of the most terrible things they started as most would abuse their power and kick or ban players for no reason)
-	A.I voice moderation (players including me would constantly get banned for talking about basic things)

I could go on and on about so many more issues with how this company treated this game, but for the reasons above and more, I no longer can play this game anymore due to company practices and the game just not being good and as fun as it use to be. I would have definitely recommended this game to someone around 2019-2023 but no longer can.

I don't know why the indie games always have the most delusional reviews but this game has very glaring issues. 

Firstly, the new player experience is horrendous if you want to co-op. Every low level area is a ghost town, with most players being hundreds or even a thousand levels above you. But even when they invite you, there's a 50% chance the invite won't pop up on your screen, forcing one or both players to relog.

Don't let the $5 price tag fool you, this game is pay to win. The best summons are pay ONLY. You can pay for double item drop chance, double gem regain, exp(level cap is 1500 lol). You can even pay $150 for a starting character with custom abilities..... Even most mobile gacha games aren't this bad.
-Spend 100+ USD on game..Disagree with Dev(which sounds more like a super ego kid) and have your account deleted :D

The AI-written intro song about joining your discord and ripping off other games is annoying.
The game let me create a username of "null" - which I find absolutely hilarious.
There is no local save, and requires a login on some third party system (but that also doesn't tie to Steam, so you can make as many accounts as you want).
A lot of text is unreadable and overlays itself.
The reason there are people with hundreds of hours in the game is because idling gives you "diamonds" which is a premium purchase. These are quite predatory - even for a mobile game.  The developer is using "playtime" to show how good their game is in Facebook groups - while hilariously some of that playtime is 22-24 hours/day for weeks.
There's a "cheat destroyer" NPC that does nothing but take you to your Discord, but tells you that botting isn't considered cheating because you've implemented "anti-bot" methods. (yeah, ok...)
There are two language options, Brazilian Portuguese and English - and English is generated with ChatGPT.
It's been out for a year and a half and hasn't broken a player count of 50, but averaging under 20.

More of a slot machine than a video game.

Insanely P2W if you don't want to spend literal thousands of hours grinding out fights for "diamonds".  There's a reason the game is being given out for free: whale hunting.

People play what looks to be a great concept of a game (multiplayer jrpg, sounds amazing!).  But you don't find out till you're some ways in that if you want to get your character as strong as possible, you're looking at spending several HUNDREDS of dollars or several THOUSAND hours of grinding out the premium currency needed for upgrades.  No exaggeration.  If any doubt about what I say, just look into the premium currencies/services for sale through in game or discord.

I don't know exactly what game could get me to drop several hundred dollars, but this ain't the one.  It's a shame that it looks so promising but becomes indistinguishable from a typical mobile gatcha game.

And to anyone that disagrees with ANYTHING posted above, please say what exactly you find to be false.  I find it ludicrous that anyone can describe the game as "not at all p2w" when you are literally given the choice of grinding 1,000+ hours or simply paying $200 if you wish to fully upgrade a character...not p2w my @$$.

I played the first series of Final Fantasy (around 1-5), Dragon Quest series, Pokemon Red/Yellow/Gold/Silver etc, when I was still around 9-14 years old. Fast forward, I'm now 35 years old and this game brings the nostalgia and longing when I was still a kid. Imbued with MMO elements, gacha taming monsters and its old school RPJ style - game is great on its ways.

However, one thing that hinders me on giving a positive review are the following:

Pay for Convenience/Fast Progress
-No, game is NOT technically Pay to Win. Though I got the game for free, it still has pay-to-gacha elements. You can still earn the red gems in game but not much. You can pay real money for more red gems to be able to summon more souls, hence, faster progress. I don't mind paying certain amounts to help the devs if we're just going to pay for cosmetics and such. 

Stuck at Lava Mines quest
-I've been circling around the Lava Mines and have no where else, no idea where to go. So my progress was stuck at this point.

A few suggestions:

Questing
-Though the quest is linear, still no menu how to keep track like if its completed/done or still in progress. Got the quest item, but when I went back to the Quest NPC, he/she is nowhere to be found.
-Quest NPC should have a status icon like Yellow "!" if not yet started, Grayed "!" if in progress then "?" if completed.

Souls Companion Filter keys
-Simply to filter the souls such as: Tiers/Stars, Element, etc.

Protagonist Avatar and customization.
-First hour playing the game, got confused of the relationship of my character and the main protagonist due to differing avatars. I just realized we're just one as I progress the story. Would be nice if my character can be customized and it's avatar would be the one to be featured via the storyline play.

Shining Chests/Objects/Areas
-Just a little animation showing that a certain object/area can be triggered.

Show Enemy Stats during combat
-This would give players strategy which moves are needed to kills mobs easily such as what was the mob's element etc.

Still hoping the game would turn out great in the future.

Lost Ark has excellent combat, flashy visuals, and a ridiculous amount of content, but actually getting into the game is an ordeal. New players are immediately buried under systems, currencies, menus, events, engravings, cards, gems, and time limited mechanics with very little meaningful onboarding to explain why any of it matters. The early game rushes you toward endgame while simultaneously expecting you to understand years’ worth of layered progression. Missed events, outdated guides, and constant FOMO make it feel like you’re already behind before you even start. Even basic progression often requires external guides, spreadsheets, or community explanations just to avoid wasting resources. Once you’re in, Lost Ark can be rewarding but the barrier to entry is so high that it actively pushes away new and returning players. If you weren’t there early or don’t want to treat learning the game like a part time job, this is a hard sell despite how good the core gameplay is.

Still get upset when I think about this game.

What makes it worse is that none of this was a surprise. Everyone knew exactly how this would play out in the West and, lo and behold, it did. Massive drop-off, dwindling relevance, and a game remembered more for its monetization than its potential.

Such a waste.

Wow, what a mess this is. While it has potential fundamentally, it just tries to be too many things at the same time, totally losing any identity and watering down every aspect.
The game is solid from a technical perspective. While it doesn't do anything mind-blowing, it mostly runs well and looks good. I never encountered any major bugs, crashes or freezes. It features a vast, mostly beautifully designed world and an interesting premise for a good narrative. Enemy encounters and set pieces are sometimes spectacular. 
But... it just throws such a huge pile of mechanics and systems at you, that it all ends up being a complete chaos fest. Whenever you start the game, you are greeted by windows upon windows of this and that. Some of the stuff is even totally irrelevant for you, but it is still thrown at you. Thousand of packs, items, events, rewards etc. Here is [insert random thing]. OK. And here is [insert another random thing]. OK.
20 minutes later I am done just clicking through that stuff, and by then I sometimes lost all interest in even playing the game, because each start of it is just exhausting.
The UI is incoherent and unintuitive, there is no clear design to all the windows, and functions, as if each one was designed by a different person and just thrown into the game to have "more stuff". 
Of course you have more skills, buffs, levels, gear, equipment, enchantments etc. than you could ever keep track of. Of course you can build up a whole island and have a game within the game there. Of course you complete a bazillion stupid fetch quests which are basically identical. Of course you can have emotes, play music and cook. Of course there are thousands of character cards and hidden collectibles, which give tiny bonuses. Of course you can have a huge number of pets and mounts. It. Is. Just. Too. Much. Of. Everything.
And the problem with the sheer amount of stuff is that most of it is half-baked and poorly explained.
Why the hell is only the very first sentence of each conversation voiced?
Why the hell are some items forever stuck in my inventory without any way to get rid of them?
Why the hell is is such a pain to play in a party with friends? 
Why the hell aren't there more well written quests instead of all the slop?
I could go on and on... and I plan to at least finish the main questline, as I generally hate stopping midgame. But never have the words "less is more" been more fitting than in this case.

A Beautiful Disaster Wrapped in RNG and Outdated Design

Lost Ark is, without exaggeration, one of the most frustrating MMORPG experiences I’ve ever had. It’s a game built on stunning visuals and flashy combat, yet completely suffocated by some of the most predatory, toxic, and exhausting systems seen in the genre.

Let’s start with the core progression: everything is RNG. Every upgrade, every attempt at progress, every step forward is locked behind layers upon layers of randomness. Instead of feeling rewarding, progression becomes a coin flip simulator where your time, effort, and materials can simply evaporate because the game decided today isn’t your lucky day. It’s not skill-based, it’s not engaging—it’s just maddening.

And if you think you can power through it by focusing on one character, think again. Lost Ark practically forces you into maintaining 10+ alts just to funnel resources into your “main.” This isn’t optional; it’s a design choice that turns the game into a second job. The daily checklist alone feels like punishment, not entertainment.

But what really pushes Lost Ark into “unplayable” territory is the community behavior surrounding it. The obsession with gatekeeping players based on titles, achievements, or content cleared during the first week of a new patch is absurd. If you didn’t no-life the game the moment new content dropped, expect to be blacklisted, kicked from groups, or straight-up ignored. Instead of encouraging growth, the community often acts as a prison guard enforcing elitist, unrealistic standards.

In the end, Lost Ark could have been great—but its own systems are working overtime to make sure you never get to enjoy it. Between the endless RNG, the alt-character grind, and a community that punishes anyone who isn’t already at the top, the game ends up feeling less like an MMORPG and more like a chore wrapped in flashy animations.

Verdict: Beautiful on the surface, absolutely miserable underneath.

+++ If you value your time, sanity, or the concept of fun, there are far better games out there.

This game had phenomenal potential as a raiding MMO, and I'm using the past tense because I'm done coping on the possibility that Smilegate will ever improve their systems. Combat is insanely fun, class fantasy is incredibly satisfying, but it's all buried under so many layers of garbage gambling systems that Smilegate has effectively turned a diamond back into coal.

Every single interaction with progression systems feels like it was designed with malicious intent to cause the player to swipe and avoid the headache, and the western playerbase could have taken a more casual approach were it not for the system-driven FOMO compelling even free to play gamers to invest insane amounts of time to try and keep up with the average dolphin, constantly pulling the bar higher and higher for what party finder considers "an acceptably geared character".

I understand that they wished to monetize progression as their business model to keep the game free to play, but it is done in such an obscenely hostile way that you'll never get good value for your money: if we compared with other MMO competitors like WoW or FFXIV where a 14-15 bucks monthly subscription will give you access to THE ENTIRETY of the game provided you just play, spending 200 dollars a year on Lost Ark gets you nowhere. It averages out to a drop in the bottomless bucket of resources you need to continue progressing your character depending on store pack releases and gold currency exchange. Cosmetics are generally low-value or mediocre at best, but that's a matter of personal taste, I am uninterested in the KR trend of suits or formal wear skins and would much rather have good fantasy armour.

I've played this game since launch, and even swiped on occasion, but it might be time to finally give it a rest and warn people who might be unaware of it just what kind of predatory cesspool awaits them, and it will remain that way because Smilegate simply does not care about making a good game, they are perfectly content milking their dwindling playerbase until it runs dry.

I mean, you can’t even find a group for the bosses you need to fight every week to upgrade certain items, sometimes spending hours, days, or even weeks trying. Honestly, it’s one of the rare games with really great level and combat design in my eyes, but I think it will shut down eventually like New World, so I’d say don’t bother at all. I tried to understand what's going on but - no. Read its tutorial and "helper" quests or whatever they're called. It started waffling about finding an ocean going liner person - or something - and some other stuff about presets and gems or... something. Fell asleep.

Then I gave up.

It must be fate that Chris Wilson's video about Dark Patterns in games was in my recommended feed on the last day before the big update of Act 4 & The Final Day raid. I'm getting off the hamster wheel.

This game's raid combat and different classes were the only saving grace for me. In the last few weeks, I found myself enjoying solo raids on my rat alts because playing them was a breath of fresh air compared to running the same 6 gold earners for the 9000th time. I enjoyed my time with the game playing with my friends and random people but if there's one sentence I can use to described this game is "It's as wide as the ocean and as deep as a puddle." Every older raids or activities they've introduced is abandoned because people only interact with the few latest content.

Almost every system in this game is debilitating and riddled with layers of RNG: it's by design to time gate slowing down progression. It uses so many predatory psychological manipulation like FOMO to keep you playing in the hopes that you get lucky: if you just get one good Frost/Flame Hell Key run, you'd save millions of gold from just getting a few Grudge, Keen Blunt, Cursed Doll, Raid Captain or Adrenaline books. But at the end of day, it's not going to happen. When you succeed, you don't feel good and want to do it again. You feel good that you never have to interact with that system ever again (like Transcendence or Elixirs). Designing progression systems like that makes it inevitable that your game with lose players. Old systems (Elixirs) get reworked to be easier to min-max because there's a new system that will make more money (Ark Grid). The game's reward system with the raids encourages gatekeeping by design because you want to finish your three gold earning raids ASAP so you can move on to the other 5 gold earning characters. The mokoko bootcamp event doesn't really help new players learn the raid, it only helps new players finish a raid by getting carried.

At the end of it, this game is just a predatory mobile game with a good combat/raid design. The game controls how much you get to play: everything is time-gated. It's heavily pay-to-win and has gotten worse with new content like Ark Grid and Paradise. The RNG is so egregious that it's impressive to say the least: one tapping/free tapping a weapon to +25 compared to the average cost is several millions of gold and compared to pity is probably in the tens of millions. When I found out about the Ark Grid system, I realized that this game isn't going to change. Smilegate will just nerf old systems (because it doesn't make a lot of money anymore) and introduce new systems to keep the hamsters on the wheel.

I, too, have seen the light; I've escaped and gotten off the hamster wheel. Stay away from this game and every predatory games like it. Remember that games are there for your enjoyment and played on your schedule. If you're playing on the game's schedule, it's just another job.
