I’ve found a conclusion as to how people become schizophrenic: people who play Ghosts of Tabor.

I cannot stress how annoying this game is, I have friends who recommend it to me and I try my best to like it but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ does it actually suck.
Here’s some of the most notable things I absolutely HATE about this game: 

1, For PC users when an enemy (player or bot) is medium distance away they will look like black blobs which are hard to read. 
2, Sound cues like footsteps for example will sound distant even if a player is within 25 meters of you, on top of that the bots (like scavengers) will fight eachother and their gunfire throws you off completely.
3, The tac vest and the backpack grab trigger boxes are either broken or I’m just dumb but when I go to grab my backpack, it grabs my TAC VEST. It doesn’t matter if I grab at the side of my waist or behind my shoulder, it does not help.
4, BOTS DO TOO MUCH DAMAGE, I cannot stress the amount of times a bot has wiped my team even if I am full gear, it doesn’t matter, I am getting floored. It takes half an AK MAGAZINE to kill the bot.
5, The concept of the wiping system, I understand players can just be the most valuable player but it also hinders players (like me) who don’t touch the game every day. The only way to really fix this is to improve the XP and earnings system instead of making me feel like I’ve lost everything. On top of that, it makes DLC semi-useless because people would want to collect items that work with their skins but after wipes, they just don’t appear.
6, The PC version of the game is very unoptimized which causes me to have lag spikes with my GeForce RTX 3070.
7, Weapon bullets feel extremely inaccurate, there’s nothing much to say other than the fact it feels like my bullets either don’t hit or barely damage someone I could be aiming at.
8, Weapons (like the AK-47) that require you to tilt the magazine under the gun in order to reload it tends to be very buggy and ends up making you not put the magazine in the gun, this is very apparent when you are trying to hastily reload.
9, Half of the sights in the game are either inaccurate or I am the most sped person to walk this planet, why is it that when I shoot at someone with the monocle sight with the (arguably weird) crosshair, it doesn’t even sound like it hit them, doesn’t matter whether it’s full-auto or not, it will not register a hit for me for some reason.
10, The on-screen effects PISS ME OFF, if I am low health, I would like atleast a sound cue, but no. I have to have a red ring covering 75% of the screen after I’ve been shot twice. On top of that, the heartbeat sound cue is LOUD.

That’s my finishing thoughts, I have a handful of friends who want me to play this game and I absolutely hate it.

Ghosts of Tabor - ⭐⭐½☆☆ (2.5/5 Stars)

“Even Rome fell, eventually.”
That’s the only honest way to start this cluster ♥♥♥♥.

I put 70 hours into Ghosts of Tabor on PCVR with a Quest 3 and custom setup. For a while, this game absolutely earned it's keep. Ghosts of Tabor started out genuinely amazing. Great guns. Real tension. Nerve-wracking trust games with strangers. A grim, lawless setting that made every extraction feel earned. It was immersive, punishing, and thrilling in the way only VR can be when it actually works.

And then… it just stopped getting better.
Worse, it started actively getting worse.

I consider this a classic story about a good game collapsing under its own mismanagement.

⭐ Core Design & Vision - 1/1

The idea is solid. Still is. A VR extraction shooter where loss matters, paranoia is constant, and survival depends on your decisions? That’s gold. The dystopian setting works. The tone works. The premise works. For a time, the developers were attentive. The community was growing naturally. The rough edges felt like early access growing pains, not warning signs. This was a game worth investing in.

That’s what makes everything else hurt more.

⭐ Gameplay & Mechanics - 0.5/1

When Ghosts of Tabor works, it’s fantastic. Gun handling feels great. Reloads are tactile. Firefights typically scare the piss out of you randomly. Clearing rooms with friends or cautiously trusting a stranger never stops being intense.

But the keyword there is when. Crashes mid-raid. VR tracking failures. Systems just… stopping. And in an extraction shooter, that’s unforgivable, because failure is losing your ♥♥♥♥. Like they said in Ready Player One, "Losing your ♥♥♥♥ means, well... losing your ♥♥♥♥."

When you lose gear, it has to be your fault. Here, too often, it isn’t.

⭐ Immersion & Atmosphere - 0.5/1

The immersion is strong until it shatters. The world feels hostile and empty in the right way. The bunker used to feel like home. The tension of moving through Tabor with a full bag and a bad feeling? Still unmatched.

But immersion dies the moment:
Loot doesn’t save
VOIP doesn’t work
Friends lists bug out
The game freezes while you’re still alive in-raid

Nothing pulls you out of VR faster than realizing your loss had nothing to do with skill. It all to do with the lack of care that a game is receiving.

⭐ Stability & Respect for the Player - 0/1

This is where the game collapses completely and utterly.

Weekly server issues. Cloud-hosted infrastructure that clearly can’t handle the load. Progress failing to save. Crashes that delete hours of effort. And somehow... SOMEHOW... new microtransactions and partnered DLCs still roll out. W, T, F. That’s the part that kills any goodwill. You can’t ask players to accept brutal punishment while your game randomly eats their progress. You can’t sell premium DLC while core systems barely function. And you definitely can’t brush off criticism while posting brainrot shorts instead of addressing real problems. (I'm looking at you, Viewie.)

⭐ Emotional Impact & Legacy - 0.5/1

This should have been one of VR’s defining games. Instead, it’s a cautionary tale of how easily you can stray from the path. What hurts most is this: I still sometimes play it. Not to progress, or to grind. Just to mess around with friends, knowing full well nothing I do really matters. I usually play 1-5 rounds per wipe, and leave.

Ghosts of Tabor didn’t become bad. It became untrustworthy.

Final Verdict - ⭐⭐½☆☆ (2.5/5 Stars)

Ghosts of Tabor is a very good idea trapped inside a very broken game. The gunplay is strong. The immersion can be incredible. The core concept still works. But none of that matters when the game routinely disrespects your time, deletes your progress, and prioritizes monetization over stability.

I can still have fun with it... but I cannot recommend it. Not in this state.

Rome didn’t fall because the idea was bad.
It fell because the foundation cracked, and no one fixed it.

[*]Fix your janky visual glitches especially with important things like AMMUNITION, where checking chambers or loading magazines might sometimes not visually show what you're expecting as a player

I cannot recommend this game in any capacity. This game, for the past 2, almost 3 years of me popping in and out to see it grow has only shown a decrease in quality. The audio, the gun mechanics, the physics, the movement, the graphics, even the tutorial was just... god awful. I found myself struggling and fumbling over my hands trying to just get simple tasks done that Contractors Showdown has just wiped the floor with. I am amazed that people put up with this game and its jank, still continuing to support the devs while they seem to not even focus on the core mechanics of the game. I was just amazed at how playing the game felt like i was still playing the same pre-release version from 2023 with just more bloat and more 'hardcore!1!' mechanics. 

I honestly wanted to give this game another fair shot, being an  extraction-only game, but oh my. Contractors has clearly showed with a solid foundation behind the game mode it can actually be done well (and fun!). I really *really* wanted to have fun with this game again, being late 2025 and not playing since earlier in the year, and realizing that nothing has changed. This game had amazing potential with my initial thoughts being "oh yeah they'll fix this" with nothing being changed. It still looks like the same assets used over and over. VAIL extraction recently released as well with literally proving to me, personally, that a solid gunplay foundation makes any damn mode playable, even if its running at 75% of my refresh rate. With the games soul purpose being extraction I expected something. Something that would actually stand out amongst the rest but no, I'm only left with the worst feeling guns out of any-ANY VR shooter out now. I would genuinely rather fumble over onward's V-stock for the 7 hours i wasted on this game, and the $30 or so dollars I spent as a young impressionable boy. 

So, as I can say these are my recommendations if you want a VR 'Extraction' experience in VR. 

for the easiest-simple recommendation is Contractor$: Showdown Exfilzone, it and C$S, built off of the Original C$, have a insanely solid foundation for VR Shooters, with C$S being one of the original VR-Esport shooters and having that previous experience to create an amazing foundation. But even then, its NOT something i can recommend at the same time, caveman studios has abandoned their original title and even the Battle Royale has seen 0 changes since their "season 1" release, with probably the most FUN VR BR i have ever touched, mixing the amazing arcade mechanics of C$ and a touch of realism with the guns and graphics, separating from POP1. Back on track, the developers just... abandon projects... out of nowhere it seems and it really worries me for the future of this game. 7/10 overall for exfilzone, 4/10 for caveman. 

VAIL: Extraction is AEXLAB's next step with their OG game VAIL VR // VAIL: Combat. It seems... promising? AEX is a unique developer creating possibly the most immersive, best, most amazing 10/10 guns I have ever used in a VR game. Not in the H3VR way, but in the "Wow! I can pick up, use this gun, and have ZERO! yes, ZERO! issues with!" and they still have that touch of realism that I can't get enough of. If I was basing this review off of "games with good core mechanics that could make good x game" this would be the 10/10 review. Literally any, ANY genre with VAIL's guns would be my own personal dream game. Forefront? with VAIL's guns? 10/10, but this isn't the point. VAIL has set their sights on the extraction bag and want to cash in on the gamemode. Their 'early access' release is out now and it being free to play i can't *not* recommend it, but it has major flaws with the gameplay and is fairly buggy. (but not to the extent of tabor! like what?!) With the map having unfinished areas and some issues just being a fault of the mode being so so early on. this is def something I'd keep my eye on, but again, its VAIL so if you do step in, you will get crushed by a mega-tryhard who has nothing better to do but ruin your night. (VAIL was initially built to be a competitive ESports title, so..) 

Overall, I am just dumbfounded by the state Ghosts Of Tabor finds itself in. Like seriously. This game feels like they took "gun model AK47" and "Free Gunshot Sound Effects Library: Royalty Free Assets" and slapped them together to create a game that closely copied Tarkov in the worst ways possible. It is so painfully obvious the developers have maybe touched a VR headset once or twice-ever, with it feeling like they focus more and more on the DLC's than the actual mechanics their game is based off of. One of the most popular VR games and for the only reason being its the *only* VR game like it that still stands alone. Caveman have the sword to strike, but knowing that team it's only going to end back up into themselves. VAIL and AEX are too different and are catering to a different type of player. Posting this as a yearn for a time where Combat Waffle, filled with what I can assume are amazing people, really actually take this review as a sign that I want this game to be good. No bias with what other games I stand by and play (knowing the drama with other studios). Seriously read this and understand that I have only seen you as a studio go and say "skill issue" (with it being funny) and bash other studios and games relentlessly without realizing your own game is an absolute mess. 7 hours may not be a lot of time, but it was the only time I was willing to put up with the gross feel, audio, graphics, UI, etc etc. I was absolutely amazed to learn the developers are actually fairly familiar with the weapons and the inclusion of the safety switch (cool detail) was nice, but to really end all of the detail at that with the scopes looking like red paint on a glass surface was weird. especially after seeing VAIL and their zoomed scopes (running on quest as well btw) was just like "wow this looks disgusting". the ONE thing i found myself actually enjoying was the night vision. Good job one developer who made that 2 years ago. There is nothing here in this game if you want to try a good VR extraction shooter, there isn't really anything. If you want a GOOD VR Experience, you will need to wait until another studio is willing to make it. Triangle Factory are making their rounds through the "Blank but in VR" games, so give it 2 more years and maybe we'll finally get a decent Extraction gamemode. (or maybe AEXLAB pull through and make theirs amazing!) 

Who really knows anymore. 1/10 overall. 1 point because the night vision was really just that cool. Not H3VR cool, but cooler than most of the other games! cough cough onward cough cough.

I'll just request a refund for now and see if this game is worth playing if it ever reaches 1.0.

The core concept is very cool. Being able to control infantry, tanks, and ships directly and walk around inside your bases and ships gives an incredible sense of scale. It’s really cool seeing how massive everything feels. I also like that you can interact with your base through in-game terminals, even if their placement can be very "uncreative".

Unfortunately, the execution feels very unpolished right now.

The UI is not very responsive and often gives little or no feedback when you issue commands, so i'm left wondering if something actually worked. Many ship icons look almost identical, which makes it hard to quickly find important units like builders among the other ships. Performance is also surprisingly poor, and the camera feels clunky... I often feel like I’m fighting it just to look around.

Key base information like economy, production, tech, and power is buried in menus and difficult to access quickly when you need it.

Ship combat also feels awkward. Ships have to get almost point-blank before engaging, which looks and feels wrong... combat range should be increased. Combat overall also feels heavily space-focused: once you land on an enemy asteroid, the match is effectively decided. You can lock them in and destroy their space economy with little chance of recovery at that stage, which makes ground combat feel less meaningful.

In the original Executive Assault, I could create specialized unit loadouts with different weapons. That either seems removed as I cannot find it now, which is a strange downgrade. The tech tree is large but you always go down the same route every time, so every round feels very similar. But there are three distinct factions which cater to slightly different playstyles, which is nice

There are also no clear alerts when your ships are attacked or destroyed, I have no idea why this is the case...

Overall:
There’s a lot of potential here. The concept is great, but the game needs significant polish and usability improvements before I can recommend it. It can still be fun in co-op against a friend or AI, but it’s hard to recommend in its current state.

Buggy, slow performance, clunky and unintuitive. Developer is also pinging their discord, to review bomb and move it away from "Mostly Negative" in recent reviews, with some people offering to review it on several of their alt accounts.

So the premise of this game is actually pretty cool. The issue is the way it is implemented. You can build some really cool ships and upgrade their weapons as we would expect, but nearly every weapon has such a short range that you cannot utilise your ships. Not to mention the pathing for the units is god awful, they get in the way of each other, block each other, sometimes you order them to move and they just dont do anything. Combine this with most ship on ship fights always being point blank means that you cannot effectively use more than a few ships at a time which makes engagements with the enemy seem very linear because outflanking just isnt a thing due to the way ships path, or cant path to be exact. Also the AI enemies you fight against dont seem to have to worry about resources and just pump out a dozen ships and send them straight to your doorstep in the first 15 mins of a match. I have also noticed the AI just doesnt build fighters or bombers in any significant quantity, just large ships. Also the idea of using power for your base as a finite resource seems cool, but in reality its just annoying because you can only build a single power production facility, and once you do, you have just enough power to build the basic structures and a tiny bit left over for defences, of which you have to build 4 different types. Also the graphics seem off somehow, i cant exactly say why, but things just look off, like the art style between your base structures and ships dont match up quite right. I wished I could slap this game with a neutral review, but sadly I tend to think negatively of this game. I bought this game a couple years ago, it looked quite cool. The game was short, it was moderately fun, and the sole gore was part of the entertainment, back then I would have given it a thumbs up for being an early access after all. Fast forward to the present day and... Nothing has really changed. Sure, the game looks prettier, it has more languages, a couple more spells but story wise we still have the same handful of levels and, for the little that I saw in my short paused 2nd playthrough, no real changes or improvements. 
Now, I really like the idea that the devs have in mind: A small 2d shooter with randomized events and key-search (potentially leading to some micro-roguelite) sounds fun, even if for playing for little more than a hoof-ful of hours for at the price of pocket change, but the problem lays on the progress of development that doesn't compensate for the few cons this game has. The game is incomplete as they have stated, and the lack of levels and clunky start make it hard to look forward to making progress, as the lack of weapons from the 1st level makes a very boring struggle of fighting the sprited hitboxes because the only way you can get through the tight corridors is by running into enemies, baiting them into an open (but still tight) area and surrounding them to get ahead of them, and their short attention span makes you run back and forth to be able to achieve it. Health mechanics are also a nice touch despite their simplicity, definitely more interesting than only having a physical-state health and personal hell gives you a small break if you died to a tight situation and gives you the chance to get some better PSYCH for when you return. Again, problem is that all the fancy and cutesy experimental things are obscured by being the same game it was two years ago with zero relevant additions, and even like this, the devs have decided that it's a good idea to set a prequel that, despite looking fun and interesting with the same feeling of D'Lirium, is very likely going to get itself stuck into never getting significant additions if it happens.
Of course I'm a brony, and of course I enjoy me some colourful little horses on the store page, but as much as I'd love to support this game, I can't recommend something that won't be done in a decade and dares to branch out to another project either way.

Developed and published by Bambuseae Games, Starlight is a low effort casual game with additional adult content.

Even though it was Released in 2019 this game is near identical in style to the various free adult flash games that were released in the late 90’s /early 2000s on Newgrounds and other flash sites, providing too like content to even the cheapest of price tags. 
With only ten levels not including adult scenes the player is required to click on 5 golden coloured falling stars to proceed to the next scene, whilst there are other coloured stars these do nothing except for achievement purposes. The entire game can be completed in under a minute and takes between 15 to 30 minutes to complete all the achievements depending on the spawn luck of several colours. 

[h1]Story [/h1]

Nonexistent, no story or plot is provided at all.

[h1]Presentation[/h1]

While the artwork is mediocre, its not terrible but suffers from been generic and very tame in the adult scenes. The animated adult scenes are hampered by poor animation with glaring obvious errors in several of them and are simple swaying in the rest. 
The music quality is surprising decent and varied but given the short nature of each level and the fact the track changes between levels this design choice actually acts against the game as you never get more than a minute at most of each track and the frequent changing becomes grating on the ears.

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]

In a word clunky, adding a few more tedious and uninspired. This is basically a tech demo, someone learning how to create a game with their tool set and it shows. This should be a free game on Bambuseae Games website and not on Steam for any price.

If there was a neutral review on steam I would rate this as "neutral", but since there isn't i'm going to go with "no" since that's where I lean to more.

The pros:

- Varied environments: lush jungles, dead forests, frozen tundras, deserts, caves, crypts, etc. 
- Varied enemy types: slow heavy mobs, fast small mobs, archers, mages and melee, including epic boss fights. These are separated to animals, undead and monsters, all with different weaknesses and attack patterns.
- Varied spells and attacks: buffs, debuffs, cc, aoe, dot, all of which open up various strategies to bring down your opponents.
- Addictive gameplay loop: micromanagement of cooldowns, range and mana and coming up with the best move each turn in a given fight makes for a satisfying and addictive gameplay
- Good graphics when compared to the old games of the genre (obviously).
- Level up is meaningful: every level up you gain spell/melee/range power based on your main attribute which you increase on level up. Every other level you also gain a perk point, which is a super powerful passive buff or active ability. This leads to every level up feeling meaningful and rewarding, and is a big difference when it comes to winning tough fights.
- The soundtrack was surprisingly good, especially the battle music. Unique boss music as well on bosses.
- Long game with lots of quests, enemies and loot.

The cons:

- Many parts of the game are pure cringe. This a result of stiff, lifeless and forgettable NPCs; bad writing; inverted map where south is up and north is down for no apparent reason; odd font type and size choices; enemy movement animations; and more.
- The story is the old cookie cutter "you're a hero that saves the world". There's nothing original about any of the plot.
- There's almost no secrets in dungeons and the world map, which are very straightforward, and also no puzzles at all. There are some chests that are a behind some statues or walls and on top of a remote hill, but that's pretty much it. This results in extremely bland exploration and dungeon design.
- UI is bad. Only 6 ability slots per char although there are dozens of abilities per char. Have to right click an enemy to see active debuffs and time remaining, instead of it hovering above their head, so you can easily forget what they have on them.
- Balance issues: there's 0 reason to take a melee attacker in this game as a ranged attacker (either caster or marksman) is far superior in power and of course it ranges out melee attacks. I had a melee defender (a.k.a tank) but he was inactive 99% of the game as almost always stuff died before it got to melee range. The game is too easy, even on hard. This is due to the imbalance issues. The Ranger is wildly OP. The wizard is a bit too strong also. A group of 3 rangers and 1 wizard (for cc) should theoretically faceroll everything in this game. Yes, there are paths that lead to fights which you should obviously not win at certain levels, but there is always one path leading to "normal", easy fights. Easily exploitable terrain (for cover), you are showered with gold (due to loot showering which you sell) which leads you to having tons of potions which are also quite op. From a certain level (about 5) most stuff died before it got to me and I was barley attacked, my tank was inactive 99% of the time. You can also respec for free and take advantage of +main attr loot to cheese out power creep after respecs. There should be a "very hard" mode in which gold and loot showering is severely impaired and a harsh limit cap on potion usage during battles.
- Map quality imbalance: the first main area got love from the dev and had several caves, several crypts, a tomb, a dead forest, 2 villages, and more. It was packed to the brim with stuff to see and do and monsters were lurking at every corner. The other maps felt open and empty in comparison.
- Game crashed more than a few times during the playthrough, sometimes during long battles I was winning, which sucked. There were areas which the game crashed consistently on, so I was unwillingly forced to leave them, but luckily they had some other path leading to them which didn't crash.
I was gonna ask who on earth balanced this but its pretty evident this step was not a part of the process.

The core type-of-puzzle concept is a nice one. The art and animation is cute and fun. And... it works? But it doesn't have that satisfying "click" of "oh, *that's* how..." - at least not in the first 16 levels, which is all I did. Take this with a grain of salt, because I've been learning over the last year or two that I'm actually less of a fan of pure puzzle-games than I thought - I like there to be some element of narrative or world exploration.

Still, I found some of the design decisions here a little baffling: "hint" seems to be "show you exactly how to solve this puzzle", which isn't really what I think of as a "hint". You can't access the trickier "Red/Blue" version of each level (where you have to get each star with a specific charge) until you've beaten the less-interesting generic version. There's this latency between allowed clicks so that even if you can see the exact sequence you need to perform, you can't just *do* it, you have to wait this flow-disrupting moment or two to click and then wait for the animation and tiny delay before you can click again. You can earn spins on a wheel to get hints (which are so potent I don't want to use them) and ?lives? and... more spins. "Clicking a button, waiting for an animation, and finding that you've earned the ability to do the exact same thing again" isn't exactly the height of fun. (But at least for all the levels I played through, you can just ignore the Wheel, so at least that's good?)

I also wonder why there wasn't a simple "# of moves" counter, with a "lowest moves" kept track of for each level. Even with no requirement or benchmark, it would add an optional form of challenging yourself to be efficient, which might have given the earlier levels a little more interest - as is, it's often really easy to isolate the charges and move one at a time, which is effective and a little boring. I'd have liked even a mild incentive to be clever with moving both at once.

Secondly.... if I wanted to date someone that Ugly I would date in Real Life; This is suppose to be a Fantasy Experience; No Man Fantasizes about dating a woman that looks like this.  The Avatar Photos are very nice; but once generated... Well, She has a real nice personality though! It's Virtual... and supposedly the Lady Avatars are Computer Generated; so since they aren't real anyways... I'd rather have a Hot, Sexy CGI Virtual Lady to Pretend to Date.

And lastly... Repetitive Mini-Games to earn money to purchase Skimpy Outfits; Outfits would almost be worth it, if I could purchase a bag for over her face :(.  However... the Dance Mini-Game is a bit Challenging and Fun.

And while I cannot recommend this game; it does have one rather Cool Feature (which I don't recall seeing advertised on the story page (Though I don't always read everything)). The ability to take your Uggo Virtual Girl to your Desktop; and have her sit there and dance while you do other things. Well, other things on your other monitors, if you're set up with Multiple monitors.  She doesn't remain in the Foreground... and she won't leave your main monitor; but otherwise... cool feature.

I won't ask for a Refund, because it does work (Crashed Once, but seems 90% stable); and I don't see myself spending much time on it.  But more than likely will end up UnInstalling to free up DiskSpace.

Catch It On Sale... under $5 ($0.99 if possible)... or in a Bundle with, at least, 1 Item you know you want; and you can live with it.
its 4/10
